***Theravāda[[a]](#cite_note-3)*** is [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism")'s oldest existing school.[[1]](#cite_note-Bodhi-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Britannica-2) The school's adherents, termed ***Theravādins*** ([anglicized](/wiki/Anglicized "Anglicized") from [Pali](/wiki/Pali "Pali") *theravādī*),[[3]](#cite_note-4)[[4]](#cite_note-5) have preserved their version of [the Buddha](/wiki/The_Buddha "The Buddha")'s teaching or *[Dhamma](/wiki/Dharma_(Buddhism) "Dharma (Buddhism)")* in the [Pāli Canon](/wiki/P%C4%81li_Canon "Pāli Canon") for over two millennia.[[1]](#cite_note-Bodhi-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Britannica-2)[[web 1]](#cite_note-Braun2014-6) As of 2010, Theravada is the second largest branch of Buddhism with 36% Buddhists belonging to Theravada, compared to 53% to Mahayana.[[5]](#cite_note-7)

The Pāli Canon is the most complete Buddhist canon surviving in a [classical Indian language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages"), [Pāli](/wiki/P%C4%81li "Pāli"), which serves as the school's [sacred language](/wiki/Sacred_language "Sacred language")[[2]](#cite_note-Britannica-2) and *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")*.[[6]](#cite_note-Crosby,_Kate_2013_p._2-8) In contrast to [Mahāyāna](/wiki/Mah%C4%81y%C4%81na "Mahāyāna") and [Vajrayāna](/wiki/Vajray%C4%81na "Vajrayāna"), Theravāda tends to be conservative in matters of the theoretical study (*[pariyatti](/wiki/Pariyatti "Pariyatti")*) of the doctrine (*Dhamma*) and monastic discipline (*[Vinaya](/wiki/Vinaya "Vinaya")*).[[7]](#cite_note-9) One element of this [conservatism](/wiki/Religious_conservatism "Religious conservatism") is the fact that Theravāda rejects the authenticity of the [Mahayana sutras](/wiki/Mahayana_sutras "Mahayana sutras") (which appeared c. 1st century BCE onwards).[[8]](#cite_note-10)[[9]](#cite_note-11) Consequently, Theravāda generally does not recognize the existence of many Buddhas and [bodhisattvas](/wiki/Bodhisattva "Bodhisattva") believed by the Mahāyāna school, such as [Amitābha](/wiki/Amit%C4%81bha "Amitābha") and [Vairocana](/wiki/Vairocana "Vairocana"), because they are not found in their scriptures.[[10]](#cite_note-Williams-2008f-12)

Theravāda derives from [Indian](/wiki/India "India") [Sthavira nikāya](/wiki/Sthavira_nik%C4%81ya "Sthavira nikāya") (an [early Buddhist school](/wiki/Early_Buddhist_schools "Early Buddhist schools")). This tradition developed significantly in [India](/wiki/India "India") from the 3rd century BCE onward, particularly with the writing down of the Pāli Canon and the growth of its [commentarial](/wiki/Atthakatha "Atthakatha") literature.[[11]](#cite_note-13)[[12]](#cite_note-14)[[13]](#cite_note-15) From both India, as its historical origin, and Sri Lanka, as its principal center of development, the Theravāda tradition subsequently [spread to Southeast Asia](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Southeast_Asia "Buddhism in Southeast Asia"), where it became the dominant form of Buddhism.[[14]](#cite_note-16) Theravāda is the official religion of Sri Lanka, [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"), and [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia"), and the main dominant Buddhist variant found in [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos") and [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"). It is practiced by minorities in India, [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), [China](/wiki/China "China"), [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal"), [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") and [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), as well as by [diaspora](/wiki/Diaspora "Diaspora") from all these groups and converts from around the world.

During the modern era, new developments have included [Buddhist modernism](/wiki/Buddhist_modernism "Buddhist modernism"), the [Vipassana movement](/wiki/Vipassana_movement "Vipassana movement") which reinvigorated Theravāda meditation practice,[[web 1]](#cite_note-Braun2014-6) the growth of the [Thai Forest Tradition](/wiki/Thai_Forest_Tradition "Thai Forest Tradition") which reemphasized forest monasticism and the spread of Theravāda westward to places such as India and Nepal, along with Buddhist immigrants and converts in the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The Pali term *Theravāda* translates to "Doctrine of the Elders"; *thera* (Sanskrit *[sthavira](/wiki/Sthavira "Sthavira")*) means "elder" or "senior monk", while *vāda* means "doctrine", "teaching" or "school". The term references the school's asserted roots in the [early Buddhist](/wiki/Early_Buddhist "Early Buddhist") [Sthavira nikāya](/wiki/Sthavira_nik%C4%81ya "Sthavira nikāya").

According to [Kate Crosby](/wiki/Kate_Crosby "Kate Crosby"), in "the [Mahāvihāra in Anuradhapura](/wiki/Anuradhapura_Maha_Viharaya "Anuradhapura Maha Viharaya") in Sri Lanka in the fifth century CE ... the term *thera-vāda* was used to refer to the teachings of ... those senior monks who gathered [at] “the [first council](/wiki/First_Buddhist_council "First Buddhist council")”." This usage attributed the Mahāvihāra's own canonical codifications and commentaries of the Tipitaka to the Buddha's immediate [arahant](/wiki/Arahant "Arahant") disciples. "In the twelfth century, the Mahāvihāra monastic tradition came to dominate the Buddhism of Sri Lanka and [from there] Southeast Asia".[[15]](#cite_note-17)

But it "is only in the modern period that the term *thera-vāda* ... came to be equated with the community religion of this region, becoming its official designation at the [World Fellowship of Buddhists](/wiki/World_Fellowship_of_Buddhists "World Fellowship of Buddhists") in 1950".[[16]](#cite_note-18) Colonial-era scholarship of the 19th and 20th centuries initially called this tradition [Southern Buddhism](/wiki/Southern_Buddhism "Southern Buddhism"), associating it with "the 222 doctrinal theses claimed as “orthodox” in the [Abhidhamma](/wiki/Abhidhamma "Abhidhamma") book, the [Kathāvatthu](/wiki/Kath%C4%81vatthu "Kathāvatthu")". This "led to it being labeled as one of the [Hīnayāna](/wiki/H%C4%ABnay%C4%81na "Hīnayāna") “inferior vehicle” schools, a pejorative term found in [Mahayana sūtras](/wiki/Mahayana_s%C5%ABtra "Mahayana sūtra") to refer to the Buddhism of the opponents... It was in reaction to this identification that representatives... from the end of the nineteenth century" sought an acceptable common label, ultimately settling on "Theravada".[[17]](#cite_note-19)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Theravāda Buddhism](/wiki/History_of_Therav%C4%81da_Buddhism "History of Theravāda Buddhism")

### Pre-modern

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Pre-modern")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Thuparamaya_Stupa_and_Stone_Pillars.jpg/250px-Thuparamaya_Stupa_and_Stone_Pillars.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thuparamaya_Stupa_and_Stone_Pillars.jpg)

The [Thuparamaya](/wiki/Thuparamaya "Thuparamaya") Stupa, the earliest [stupa](/wiki/Stupa "Stupa") after Theravada Buddhism became the official religion in [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), dating back to the reign of King [Devanampiya Tissa](/wiki/Devanampiya_Tissa "Devanampiya Tissa") (247–207 BCE)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Ruwanweli_Saya_1.jpg/250px-Ruwanweli_Saya_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ruwanweli_Saya_1.jpg)

The [Ruwanwelisaya](/wiki/Ruwanwelisaya "Ruwanwelisaya") stupa, built by the Sri Lankan [King Dutugemunu](/wiki/Dutugamunu "Dutugamunu") (c. 140 BCE)

The Theravāda school descends from the [Vibhajjavāda](/wiki/Vibhajyav%C4%81da "Vibhajyavāda"), a division within the [Sthāvira nikāya](/wiki/Sthavira_nik%C4%81ya "Sthavira nikāya"), one of the two major orders that arose after the [first schism](/wiki/Second_Buddhist_council "Second Buddhist council") in the Indian Buddhist community.[[18]](#cite_note-20)[[19]](#cite_note-Cousins2001-21) Theravāda sources trace their tradition to the Third Buddhist council when elder [Moggaliputta-Tissa](/wiki/Moggaliputta-Tissa "Moggaliputta-Tissa") is said to have compiled the *[Kathavatthu](/wiki/Kathavatthu "Kathavatthu")*, an important work which lays out the Vibhajjavāda doctrinal position.[[20]](#cite_note-Berkwitz2012-22)

Aided by the patronage of Mauryan kings like [Ashoka](/wiki/Ashoka "Ashoka"), this school spread throughout India and reached [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka") through the efforts of missionary monks like [Mahinda](/wiki/Mahinda_(Buddhist_monk) "Mahinda (Buddhist monk)"). In Sri Lanka, it became known as the [Tambapaṇṇiya (and later as Mahāvihāravāsins)](/wiki/Tamrashatiya "Tamrashatiya") which was based at the [Great Vihara (Mahavihara)](/wiki/Anuradhapura_Maha_Viharaya "Anuradhapura Maha Viharaya") in [Anuradhapura](/wiki/Anuradhapura "Anuradhapura") (the ancient Sri Lankan capital).[[21]](#cite_note-:0-23) According to Theravāda sources, another one of the Ashokan missions was also sent to [*Suvaṇṇabhūmi*](/wiki/Suvarnabhumi "Suvarnabhumi") ("The Golden Land"), which may refer to Southeast Asia.[[22]](#cite_note-24)

By the first century BCE, Theravāda Buddhism was well established in the main settlements of the [Kingdom of Anuradhapura](/wiki/Anuradhapura_Kingdom "Anuradhapura Kingdom").[[23]](#cite_note-25) The Pali Canon, which contains the main scriptures of the Theravāda, was committed to writing in the first century BCE.[[24]](#cite_note-26) Throughout the history of ancient and medieval Sri Lanka, Theravāda was the main religion of the [Sinhalese people](/wiki/Sinhalese_people "Sinhalese people") and its temples and monasteries were patronized by the [Sri Lankan kings](/wiki/List_of_Sri_Lankan_monarchs "List of Sri Lankan monarchs"), who saw themselves as the protectors of the religion.[[25]](#cite_note-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/TipitakaGoldPlates.jpg/250px-TipitakaGoldPlates.jpg)](/wiki/File:TipitakaGoldPlates.jpg)

Gold Plates containing fragments of the Pali Tipitaka (5th century) found in Maunggan (a village near the city of [Sriksetra](/wiki/Sri_Ksetra_Kingdom "Sri Ksetra Kingdom"))

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Old_Bagan%2C_Myanmar%2C_Bagan_plains_at_sunset.jpg/250px-Old_Bagan%2C_Myanmar%2C_Bagan_plains_at_sunset.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_Bagan,_Myanmar,_Bagan_plains_at_sunset.jpg)

[Bagan](/wiki/Bagan "Bagan"), the capital of the [Bagan Kingdom](/wiki/Bagan_Kingdom "Bagan Kingdom"). Between the 11th and 13th centuries, more than 10,000 temples, [pagodas](/wiki/Pagoda "Pagoda") and monasteries were constructed in the Bagan plains.[[26]](#cite_note-28)

Over time, two other sects split off from the Mahāvihāra tradition, the [Abhayagiri](/wiki/Abhayagiri_vih%C4%81ra "Abhayagiri vihāra") and [Jetavana](/wiki/Jetavanaramaya "Jetavanaramaya").[[27]](#cite_note-Warder,_A.K._2000._p._280-29) While the Abhayagiri sect became known for the [syncretic](/wiki/Syncretism "Syncretism") study of [Mahayana](/wiki/Mahayana "Mahayana") and [Vajrayana](/wiki/Vajrayana "Vajrayana") texts, as well as the Theravāda canon, the Mahāvihāra tradition did not accept these new scriptures.[[28]](#cite_note-30) Instead, Mahāvihāra scholars like [Buddhaghosa](/wiki/Buddhaghosa "Buddhaghosa") focused on the [exegesis](/wiki/Exegesis "Exegesis") of the Pali scriptures and on the [Abhidhamma](/wiki/Abhidharma "Abhidharma"). These Theravāda sub-sects often came into conflict with each other over royal patronage.[[29]](#cite_note-31) The reign of [Parākramabāhu I](/wiki/Parakramabahu_I "Parakramabahu I") (1153–1186) saw an extensive reform of the Sri Lankan sangha after years of warfare on the island. Parākramabāhu created a single unified sangha which came to be dominated by the Mahāvihāra sect.[[30]](#cite_note-32)[[31]](#cite_note-33)

Epigraphical evidence has established that Theravāda Buddhism became a dominant religion in the Southeast Asian kingdoms of [Sri Ksetra](/wiki/Sri_Ksetra_Kingdom "Sri Ksetra Kingdom") and [Dvaravati](/wiki/Dvaravati "Dvaravati") from about the 5th century CE onwards.[[32]](#cite_note-34) The oldest surviving Buddhist texts in the Pāli language are gold plates found at Sri Ksetra dated circa the 5th to 6th century.[[33]](#cite_note-35) Before the Theravāda tradition became the dominant religion in Southeast Asia, Mahāyāna, Vajrayana and Hinduism were also prominent.[[34]](#cite_note-36)[[35]](#cite_note-Sujato,_Bhikkhu_2006._p._72-37)

Starting at around the 11th century, Sinhalese Theravāda monks and Southeast Asian elites led a widespread conversion of most of mainland Southeast Asia to the Theravādin [Mahavihara](/wiki/Mahaviharavasin "Mahaviharavasin") school.[[36]](#cite_note-38) The patronage of monarchs such as the Burmese king [Anawrahta](/wiki/Anawrahta "Anawrahta") (Pali: Aniruddha, 1044–1077) and the Thai king [Ram Khamhaeng](/wiki/Ram_Khamhaeng "Ram Khamhaeng") ([floruit](/wiki/Floruit "Floruit"). late 13th century) was instrumental in the rise of Theravāda Buddhism as the predominant religion of Burma and Thailand.[[37]](#cite_note-39)[[38]](#cite_note-40)[[39]](#cite_note-:13-41)

Burmese and Thai kings saw themselves as [Dhamma Kings](/wiki/Buddhist_kingship "Buddhist kingship") and as protectors of the Theravāda faith. They promoted the building of new temples, patronized scholarship, monastic ordinations and missionary works as well as attempted to eliminate certain non-Buddhist practices like animal sacrifices.[[40]](#cite_note-geh-172-173-42)[[41]](#cite_note-43)[[42]](#cite_note-44) During the 15th and 16th centuries, Theravāda also became established as the state religion in Cambodia and Laos. In Cambodia, numerous Hindu and Mahāyāna temples, most famously [Angkor Wat](/wiki/Angkor_Wat "Angkor Wat") and [Angkor Thom](/wiki/Angkor_Thom "Angkor Thom"), were transformed into Theravādin monasteries.[[43]](#cite_note-45)[[44]](#cite_note-46)

### Modern history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Modern history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Buddhism-meditation-burma.jpg/250px-Buddhism-meditation-burma.jpg)](/wiki/File:Buddhism-meditation-burma.jpg)

A Burmese man meditates in [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"). The widespread practice of meditation by laypersons is a modern development in Theravāda.

In the 19th and 20th centuries, Theravāda Buddhists came into direct contact with western ideologies, religions and modern science. The various responses to this encounter have been called "[Buddhist modernism](/wiki/Buddhist_modernism "Buddhist modernism")".[[45]](#cite_note-47) In the British colonies of [Ceylon](/wiki/British_Ceylon "British Ceylon") (modern Sri Lanka) and [Burma](/wiki/British_rule_in_Burma "British rule in Burma") (Myanmar), Buddhist institutions lost their traditional role as the prime providers of education (a role that was often filled by Christian schools).[[46]](#cite_note-48) In response to this, Buddhist organizations were founded which sought to preserve Buddhist scholarship and provide a Buddhist education.[[47]](#cite_note-49) [Anagarika Dhammapala](/wiki/Anagarika_Dhammapala "Anagarika Dhammapala"), [Migettuwatte Gunananda Thera](/wiki/Migettuwatte_Gunananda_Thera "Migettuwatte Gunananda Thera"), [Hikkaduwe Sri Sumangala Thera](/wiki/Hikkaduwe_Sri_Sumangala_Thera "Hikkaduwe Sri Sumangala Thera") and [Henry Steel Olcott](/wiki/Henry_Steel_Olcott "Henry Steel Olcott") (one of the first American western converts to Buddhism) were some of the main figures of the Sri Lankan Buddhist revival.[[48]](#cite_note-50) Two new monastic orders were formed in the 19th century, the [Amarapura Nikāya](/wiki/Amarapura_Nik%C4%81ya "Amarapura Nikāya") and the [Rāmañña Nikāya](/wiki/R%C4%81ma%C3%B1%C3%B1a_Nik%C4%81ya "Rāmañña Nikāya").[[49]](#cite_note-51)

In Burma, an influential modernist figure was king [Mindon Min](/wiki/Mindon_Min "Mindon Min") (1808–1878), known for his patronage of the [Fifth Buddhist council](/wiki/Fifth_Buddhist_council "Fifth Buddhist council") (1871) and the [Tripiṭaka tablets at Kuthodaw Pagoda](/wiki/Tripi%E1%B9%ADaka_tablets_at_Kuthodaw_Pagoda "Tripiṭaka tablets at Kuthodaw Pagoda") (still the world's largest book) with the intention of preserving the Buddha Dhamma. Burma also saw the growth of the "[Vipassana movement](/wiki/Vipassana_movement "Vipassana movement")", which focused on reviving Buddhist meditation and [doctrinal learning](/wiki/Pariyatti "Pariyatti"). [Ledi Sayadaw](/wiki/Ledi_Sayadaw "Ledi Sayadaw") (1846–1923) was one of the key figures in this movement.[[50]](#cite_note-insight-52) After independence, Myanmar held the [Sixth Buddhist council](/wiki/Sixth_Buddhist_council "Sixth Buddhist council") ([Vesak](/wiki/Vesak "Vesak") 1954 to Vesak 1956) to create a new redaction of the [Pāli Canon](/wiki/P%C4%81li_Canon "Pāli Canon"), which was then published by the government in 40 volumes. The Vipassana movement continued to grow after independence, becoming an international movement with centers around the world. Influential meditation teachers of the post-independence era include [U Narada](/wiki/U_N%C4%81rada "U Nārada"), [Mahasi Sayadaw](/wiki/Mahasi_Sayadaw "Mahasi Sayadaw"), [Sayadaw U Pandita](/wiki/U_Pandita "U Pandita"), [Nyanaponika Thera](/wiki/Nyanaponika_Thera "Nyanaponika Thera"), [Webu Sayadaw](/wiki/Webu_Sayadaw "Webu Sayadaw"), [U Ba Khin](/wiki/Sayagyi_U_Ba_Khin "Sayagyi U Ba Khin") and his student [S.N. Goenka.](/wiki/S._N._Goenka "S. N. Goenka")

Meanwhile, in Thailand (the only Theravāda nation to retain its independence throughout the colonial era), the religion became much more centralized, [bureaucratized](/wiki/Bureaucracy "Bureaucracy") and controlled by the state after a series of reforms promoted by Thai kings of the [Chakri dynasty](/wiki/Chakri_dynasty "Chakri dynasty"). King [Mongkut](/wiki/Mongkut "Mongkut") (r. 1851–1868) and his successor [Chulalongkorn](/wiki/Chulalongkorn "Chulalongkorn") (1868–1910) were especially involved in centralizing sangha reforms. Under these kings, the sangha was organized into a hierarchical bureaucracy led by the [Sangha Council of Elders](/wiki/Sangha_Supreme_Council "Sangha Supreme Council") ([Pali](/wiki/Pali_language "Pali language"): *Mahāthera Samāgama*), the highest body of the Thai sangha.[[51]](#cite_note-:14-53) Mongkut also led the creation of a new monastic order, the [Dhammayuttika](/wiki/Dhammayuttika_Nikaya "Dhammayuttika Nikaya") Nikaya, which kept a stricter monastic discipline than the rest of the Thai sangha (this included not using money, not storing up food and not taking milk in the evening).[[52]](#cite_note-:8-54)[[53]](#cite_note-55) The Dhammayuttika movement was characterized by an emphasis on the original Pali Canon and a rejection of Thai folk beliefs which were seen as irrational.[[54]](#cite_note-:18-56) Under the leadership of Prince [Wachirayan Warorot](/wiki/Vajirananavarorasa "Vajirananavarorasa"), a new education and examination system was introduced for Thai monks.[[55]](#cite_note-:15-57)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/AjahnChahSangha.jpg/250px-AjahnChahSangha.jpg)](/wiki/File:AjahnChahSangha.jpg)

Thai Forest teacher [Ajahn Chah](/wiki/Ajahn_Chah "Ajahn Chah") with [Ajahn Sumedho](/wiki/Ajahn_Sumedho "Ajahn Sumedho") (front right), [Ajahn Pasanno](/wiki/Ajahn_Pasanno "Ajahn Pasanno") (rear and left of Sumedho) and other monastics (1980)

The 20th century also saw the growth of "forest traditions" which focused on forest living and strict monastic discipline. The main forest movements of this era are the [Sri Lankan Forest Tradition](/wiki/Sri_Lankan_Forest_Tradition "Sri Lankan Forest Tradition") and the [Thai Forest Tradition](/wiki/Thai_Forest_Tradition "Thai Forest Tradition"), founded by [Ajahn Mun](/wiki/Ajahn_Mun "Ajahn Mun") (1870–1949) and his students.[[56]](#cite_note-58)

Theravāda Buddhism in Cambodia and Laos went through similar experiences in the modern era. Both had to endure French colonialism, destructive civil wars and oppressive communist governments. Under [French Rule](/wiki/French_protectorate_of_Cambodia "French protectorate of Cambodia"), French indologists of the [École française d'Extrême-Orient](/wiki/French_School_of_the_Far_East "French School of the Far East") became involved in the reform of Buddhism, setting up institutions for the training of Cambodian and Lao monks, such as the Ecole de Pali which was founded in Phnom Penh in 1914*.*[[57]](#cite_note-59) While the [Khmer Rouge](/wiki/Khmer_Rouge "Khmer Rouge") effectively destroyed Cambodia's Buddhist institutions, after the end of the communist regime the Cambodian Sangha was re-established by monks who had returned from exile.[[58]](#cite_note-Harris_2001_p=75-60) In contrast, communist rule in Laos was less destructive since the [Pathet Lao](/wiki/Pathet_Lao "Pathet Lao") sought to make use of the sangha for political ends by imposing direct state control.[[59]](#cite_note-61) During the late 1980s and 1990s, the official attitudes toward Buddhism began to liberalise in Laos and there was a resurgence of traditional Buddhist activities such as merit-making and doctrinal study.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Global_Pagoda%2CGorai%2CMeera_Rd-Bhayandar_-_panoramio_%284%29.jpg/250px-Global_Pagoda%2CGorai%2CMeera_Rd-Bhayandar_-_panoramio_%284%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Global_Pagoda,Gorai,Meera_Rd-Bhayandar_-_panoramio_(4).jpg)

[Global Vipassana Pagoda](/wiki/Global_Vipassana_Pagoda "Global Vipassana Pagoda"), Maharashtra, India. S.N. Goenka laid the foundation for the structure in 2000 and the pagoda opened in 2009. Regular meditation courses are held at the complex.

The modern era also saw the spread of Theravāda Buddhism around the world and the revival of the religion in places where it remains a minority faith. Some of the major events of the spread of modern Theravāda include:

* The 20th-century Nepalese Theravāda movement which introduced Theravāda Buddhism to [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") and was led by prominent figures such as [Dharmaditya Dharmacharya](/wiki/Dharmaditya_Dharmacharya "Dharmaditya Dharmacharya"), [Mahapragya](/wiki/Bauddha_Rishi_Mahapragya "Bauddha Rishi Mahapragya"), [Pragyananda](/wiki/Pragyananda_Mahasthavir "Pragyananda Mahasthavir") and [Dhammalok Mahasthavir](/wiki/Dhammalok_Mahasthavir "Dhammalok Mahasthavir").[[60]](#cite_note-62)
* The establishment of some of the first Theravāda Viharas in the Western world, such as the [London Buddhist Vihara](/wiki/London_Buddhist_Vihara "London Buddhist Vihara") (1926), [Das Buddhistische Haus](/wiki/Das_Buddhistische_Haus "Das Buddhistische Haus") in Berlin (1957) and the Washington Buddhist Vihara in Washington, DC (1965).
* The founding of the [Bengal Buddhist Association](/wiki/Bengal_Buddhist_Association "Bengal Buddhist Association") (1892) and the Dharmankur Vihar (1900) in [Calcutta](/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata") by the Bengali monk [Kripasaran](/wiki/Kripasaran "Kripasaran") Mahasthavir, which were key events in the Bengali Theravāda revival.[[61]](#cite_note-63)
* The founding of the [Maha Bodhi Society](/wiki/Maha_Bodhi_Society "Maha Bodhi Society") in 1891 by [Anagarika Dharmapala](/wiki/Anagarika_Dharmapala "Anagarika Dharmapala") which focused on the conservation and restoration of important Indian Buddhist sites, such as [Bodh Gaya](/wiki/Bodh_Gaya "Bodh Gaya") and [Sarnath](/wiki/Sarnath "Sarnath").[[62]](#cite_note-Jerryson,_Michael_K._p._41-64)[[63]](#cite_note-Ahir-65)
* The introduction of Theravāda to other Southeast Asian nations like Singapore, [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") and [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"). Especially with Ven. [K. Sri Dhammananda](/wiki/K._Sri_Dhammananda "K. Sri Dhammananda") missionary efforts among English-speaking Chinese communities. In addition, the establishment of the [Indonesian Theravāda Saṅgha](/wiki/Indonesian_Therav%C4%81da_Sa%E1%B9%85gha "Indonesian Theravāda Saṅgha") in 1976 and the [Theravāda Buddhist Council of Malaysia](/wiki/Theravada_Buddhist_Council_of_Malaysia "Theravada Buddhist Council of Malaysia") in 2012 also signaled the revival of Theravāda in both countries.[[64]](#cite_note-66)[[65]](#cite_note-67)
* The return of Western Theravādin monks trained in the Thai Forest Tradition to western countries and the subsequent founding of monasteries led by western monastics, such as [Abhayagiri Buddhist Monastery](/wiki/Abhayagiri_Buddhist_Monastery "Abhayagiri Buddhist Monastery"), [Chithurst Buddhist Monastery](/wiki/Chithurst_Buddhist_Monastery "Chithurst Buddhist Monastery"), [Metta Forest Monastery](/wiki/Metta_Forest_Monastery "Metta Forest Monastery"), [Amaravati Buddhist Monastery](/wiki/Amaravati_Buddhist_Monastery "Amaravati Buddhist Monastery"), [Birken Forest Buddhist Monastery](/wiki/Birken_Forest_Buddhist_Monastery "Birken Forest Buddhist Monastery"), [Bodhinyana Monastery](/wiki/Bodhinyana_Monastery "Bodhinyana Monastery") and [Santacittarama](/wiki/Santacittarama "Santacittarama").
* The spread of the [Vipassana movement](/wiki/Vipassana_movement "Vipassana movement") around the world by the efforts of people like [Mother Sayamagyi](/wiki/Mother_Sayamagyi "Mother Sayamagyi"), [S.N. Goenka](/wiki/S._N._Goenka "S. N. Goenka"), [Anagarika Munindra](/wiki/Anagarika_Munindra "Anagarika Munindra"), [Joseph Goldstein](/wiki/Joseph_Goldstein_(writer) "Joseph Goldstein (writer)"), [Jack Kornfield](/wiki/Jack_Kornfield "Jack Kornfield"), [Sharon Salzberg](/wiki/Sharon_Salzberg "Sharon Salzberg"), [Dipa Ma](/wiki/Dipa_Ma "Dipa Ma"), and [Ruth Denison](/wiki/Ruth_Denison "Ruth Denison").
* The Vietnamese Theravāda movement, led by figures such as Ven. Hộ-Tông (Vansarakkhita).[[66]](#cite_note-68)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Illustrated_Sinhalese_covers_%28inside%29_showing_the_events_Wellcome_L0031774.jpg/250px-Illustrated_Sinhalese_covers_%28inside%29_showing_the_events_Wellcome_L0031774.jpg)](/wiki/File:Illustrated_Sinhalese_covers_(inside)_showing_the_events_Wellcome_L0031774.jpg)

Pre-modern copies of the Tipiṭaka were preserved in [palm-leaf manuscripts](/wiki/Palm-leaf_manuscript "Palm-leaf manuscript"), most of which have not survived the humid climate of South Asia and Southeast Asia.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Tipitaka1.jpg/250px-Tipitaka1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tipitaka1.jpg)

A full modern set of the Tipiṭaka can fill many volumes (from 40 to over 50 volumes depending on the edition).

According to Kate Crosby, for Theravāda, the Pāli [Tipiṭaka](/wiki/Tripi%E1%B9%ADaka "Tripiṭaka"), also known as the Pāli Canon is "the highest authority on what constitutes the Dhamma (the truth or teaching of the Buddha) and the organization of the Sangha (the community of monks and nuns)."[[67]](#cite_note-69)

The language of the Tipiṭaka, [Pāli](/wiki/Pali "Pali"), is a [middle-Indic language](/wiki/Middle_Indo-Aryan_languages "Middle Indo-Aryan languages") which is the main religious and scholarly language in Theravāda. This language may have evolved out of various Indian dialects, and is related to, but not the same as, the ancient language of [Magadha](/wiki/Magadha_(Mahajanapada) "Magadha (Mahajanapada)").[[68]](#cite_note-70)

An early form of the Tipiṭaka may have been transmitted to [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka") during the reign of [Ashoka](/wiki/Ashoka "Ashoka"), which saw a period of Buddhist missionary activity. After being orally transmitted (as was the custom for religious texts in those days) for some centuries, the texts were finally committed to writing in the 1st century BCE. Theravāda is one of the first Buddhist schools to commit its Tipiṭaka to writing.[[69]](#cite_note-71) The [recension](/wiki/Recension "Recension") of the Tipiṭaka which survives today is that of the Sri Lankan Mahavihara sect.[[70]](#cite_note-72)

The oldest manuscripts of the Tipiṭaka from Sri Lanka and Southeast Asia date to the 15th Century, and they are incomplete.[[71]](#cite_note-skilling-73) Complete manuscripts of the four [Nikayas](/wiki/Nikaya "Nikaya") are only available from the 17th Century onwards.[[72]](#cite_note-analayo-74) However, fragments of the Tipiṭaka have been found in inscriptions from Southeast Asia, the earliest of which have been dated to the 3rd or 4th century.[[71]](#cite_note-skilling-73)[[73]](#cite_note-:0h-75) According to Alexander Wynne, "they agree almost exactly with extant Pāli manuscripts. This means that the Pāli Tipiṭaka has been transmitted with a high degree of accuracy for well over 1,500 years."[[73]](#cite_note-:0h-75)

There are numerous editions of the Tipiṭaka, some of the major modern editions include the [Pali Text Society](/wiki/Pali_Text_Society "Pali Text Society") edition (published in Roman script), the Burmese [Sixth Council](/wiki/Buddhist_Councils "Buddhist Councils") edition (in [Burmese script](/wiki/Burmese_alphabet "Burmese alphabet"), 1954–56) and the Thai Tipiṭaka edited and published in [Thai script](/wiki/Thai_script "Thai script") after the council held during the reign of [Rama VII](/wiki/Prajadhipok "Prajadhipok") (1925–35). There is also a [Khmer](/wiki/Khmer_script "Khmer script") edition, published in [Phnom Penh](/wiki/Phnom_Penh "Phnom Penh") (1931–69).[[74]](#cite_note-76)[[75]](#cite_note-77)[[76]](#cite_note-78)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Pali_Canon_Structure.png/330px-Pali_Canon_Structure.png)](/wiki/File:Pali_Canon_Structure.png)

The structure of the books considered canonical within the Pali Tripitaka. The Theravāda school generally only recognizes the Pali Tripitaka and rejects the authenticity of other [Tripitaka](/wiki/Tripi%E1%B9%ADaka "Tripiṭaka") versions.

The Pāli Tipitaka consists of three parts: the [Vinaya Pitaka](/wiki/Vinaya_Pitaka "Vinaya Pitaka"), [Sutta Pitaka](/wiki/Sutta_Pitaka "Sutta Pitaka") and [Abhidhamma Pitaka](/wiki/Abhidhamma_Pitaka "Abhidhamma Pitaka"). Of these, the Abhidhamma Pitaka is believed to be a later addition to the collection, its composition dating from around the 3rd century BCE onwards.[[77]](#cite_note-79) The Pāli Abhidhamma was not recognized outside the Theravāda school. There are also some texts which were late additions that are included in the fifth Nikaya, the *[Khuddaka Nikāya](/wiki/Khuddaka_Nik%C4%81ya "Khuddaka Nikāya")* ('Minor Collection'), such as the *[Paṭisambhidāmagga](/wiki/Pa%E1%B9%ADisambhid%C4%81magga "Paṭisambhidāmagga")* (possibly c. 3rd to 1st century BCE) and the *[Buddhavaṃsa](/wiki/Buddhava%E1%B9%83sa "Buddhavaṃsa")* (c. 1st and 2nd century BCE).[[78]](#cite_note-80)[[79]](#cite_note-81)

The main parts of the [Sutta Pitaka](/wiki/S%C5%ABtra "Sūtra") and some portions of the [Vinaya](/wiki/Vinaya "Vinaya") show considerable overlap in content with the [Agamas](/wiki/%C4%80gama_(Buddhism) "Āgama (Buddhism)"), the parallel collections used by non-Theravāda schools in India which are preserved in Chinese and partially in [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit"), [Prakrit](/wiki/Prakrit "Prakrit"), and [Tibetan](/wiki/Classical_Tibetan "Classical Tibetan"), as well as the various non-Theravāda Vinayas. On this basis, these [Early Buddhist texts](/wiki/Early_Buddhist_texts "Early Buddhist texts") (i.e. the Nikayas and parts of the Vinaya) are generally believed to be some of the oldest and most authoritative sources on the doctrines of [pre-sectarian Buddhism](/wiki/Pre-sectarian_Buddhism "Pre-sectarian Buddhism") by modern scholars.[[80]](#cite_note-82)[[81]](#cite_note-83)

Much of the material in the earlier portions is not specifically "Theravādan", but the collection of teachings that this school's adherents preserved from the early, non-sectarian body of teachings. According to [Peter Harvey](/wiki/Peter_Harvey_(Buddhism) "Peter Harvey (Buddhism)"), while the Theravādans may have added texts to their Tipiṭaka (such as the Abhidhamma texts and so on), they generally did not tamper with the earlier material.[[82]](#cite_note-84)

The historically later parts of the canon, mainly the Abhidhamma and some parts of the Vinaya, contain some distinctive elements and teachings which are unique to the Theravāda school and often differ from the Abhidharmas or Vinayas of other [early Buddhist schools](/wiki/Early_Buddhist_schools "Early Buddhist schools").[[83]](#cite_note-85) For example, while the Theravāda Vinaya contains a total of 227 monastic rules for [bhikkhus](/wiki/Bhikkhu "Bhikkhu"), the [Dharmaguptaka](/wiki/Dharmaguptaka "Dharmaguptaka") Vinaya (used in [East Asian Buddhism](/wiki/East_Asian_Buddhism "East Asian Buddhism")) has a total of 253 rules for bhikkhus (though the overall structure is the same).[[84]](#cite_note-86) These differences arose from the systematization and historical development of doctrines and monasticism in the centuries after the death of the Buddha.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarder2000217-87)

The Abhidhamma-pitaka contains "a restatement of the doctrine of the Buddha in strictly formalized language." Its texts present a new method, the Abhidhamma method, which attempts to build a single consistent philosophical system (in contrast with the suttas, which present numerous teachings given by the Buddha to particular individuals according to their needs).[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarder2000288-88) Because the Abhidhamma focuses on analyzing the internal lived experience of beings and the intentional structure of consciousness, it has often been compared to a kind of [phenomenological](/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy) "Phenomenology (philosophy)") [psychology](/wiki/Psychology "Psychology") by numerous modern scholars such as [Nyanaponika](/wiki/Nyanaponika_Thera "Nyanaponika Thera"), [Bhikkhu Bodhi](/wiki/Bhikkhu_Bodhi "Bhikkhu Bodhi") and [Alexander Piatigorsky](/wiki/Alexander_Piatigorsky "Alexander Piatigorsky").[[87]](#cite_note-89)

The Theravāda school has traditionally held the doctrinal position that the canonical Abhidhamma Pitaka was actually taught by the Buddha himself.[[88]](#cite_note-90) Modern scholarship in contrast, has generally held that the Abhidhamma texts date from the 3rd century BCE onwards.[[89]](#cite_note-91) However some scholars, such as [Frauwallner](/wiki/Erich_Frauwallner "Erich Frauwallner"), also hold that the early Abhidhamma texts developed out of [exegetical](/wiki/Exegesis "Exegesis") and [catechetical](/wiki/Catechesis "Catechesis") work which made use of doctrinal lists which can be seen in the suttas, called *matikas.*[[90]](#cite_note-92)[[91]](#cite_note-93)

### Non-canonical literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Non-canonical literature")]

Main article: [Pali literature](/wiki/Pali_literature "Pali literature")

There are numerous Theravāda works which are important for the tradition even though they are not part of the Tipiṭaka. Perhaps the most important texts apart from the Tipiṭaka are the works of the influential scholar [Buddhaghosa](/wiki/Buddhaghosa "Buddhaghosa") (4th–5th century CE), known for his [Pāli commentaries](/wiki/Atthakatha "Atthakatha") (which were based on older Sri Lankan commentaries of the Mahavihara tradition). He is also the author of a very important compendium of Theravāda doctrine, the *[Visuddhimagga](/wiki/Visuddhimagga "Visuddhimagga")*.[[92]](#cite_note-Crosby,_2013,_p._86-94) Other figures like [Dhammapala](/wiki/Dhammapala "Dhammapala") and [Buddhadatta](/wiki/Buddhadatta "Buddhadatta") also wrote Theravāda commentaries and other works in Pali during the time of Buddhaghosa.[[93]](#cite_note-95) While these texts do not have the same scriptural authority in Theravāda as the Tipiṭaka, they remain influential works for the [exegesis](/wiki/Exegesis "Exegesis") of the Tipiṭaka.

An important genre of Theravādin literature is shorter handbooks and summaries, which serve as introductions and study guides for the larger commentaries. Two of the more influential summaries are Sariputta Thera's *Pālimuttakavinayavinicchayasaṅgaha,* a summary of Buddhaghosa's Vinaya commentary and Anuruddha's *[Abhidhammaṭṭhasaṅgaha](/wiki/Abhidhammattha-sangaha "Abhidhammattha-sangaha")* (a "Manual of Abhidhamma").[[94]](#cite_note-Crosby,_2013,_86-96)

Throughout the history of Theravāda, Theravāda monks also produced other works of Pāli literature such as historical chronicles (like the *[Dipavamsa](/wiki/Dipavamsa "Dipavamsa")* and the *[Mahavamsa](/wiki/Mahavamsa "Mahavamsa")*), [hagiographies](/wiki/Hagiography "Hagiography"), poetry, Pāli grammars, and "[sub-commentaries](/wiki/Sub-commentaries_(Therav%C4%81da) "Sub-commentaries (Theravāda)")" (that is, commentaries on the commentaries).

While Pāli texts are symbolically and ritually important for many Theravādins, most people are likely to access Buddhist teachings through vernacular literature, oral teachings, sermons, art and performance as well as films and Internet media.[[95]](#cite_note-97) According to Kate Crosby, "there is a far greater volume of Theravāda literature in vernacular languages than in Pāli."[[96]](#cite_note-98)

An important genre of Theravādin literature, in both Pāli and vernacular languages, are the [Jataka tales](/wiki/Jataka_tales "Jataka tales"), stories of the Buddha's past lives. They are very popular among all classes and are rendered in a wide variety of media formats, from cartoons to high literature. The [Vessantara Jātaka](/wiki/Vessantara_Jataka "Vessantara Jataka") is one of the most popular of these.[[97]](#cite_note-99)

### Other Buddhist texts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Other Buddhist texts")]

Most Theravāda Buddhists generally consider [Mahāyāna](/wiki/Mahayana "Mahayana") Buddhist scriptures to be [apocryphal](/wiki/Apocrypha "Apocrypha"), meaning that they are not authentic words of the Buddha.[[98]](#cite_note-100) Consequently, Theravādin generally does not recognize the existence of many Buddhas and [bodhisattvas](/wiki/Bodhisattva "Bodhisattva") believed by the *Mahāyāna* school, such as [Amitābha](/wiki/Amit%C4%81bha "Amitābha") and [Vairocana](/wiki/Vairocana "Vairocana"), because they are not found in the canonical scriptures.[[10]](#cite_note-Williams-2008f-12)

## Doctrine (*pariyatti*)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Doctrine (pariyatti)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Sermon_in_the_Deer_Park_depicted_at_Wat_Chedi_Liem-KayEss-1.jpeg/250px-Sermon_in_the_Deer_Park_depicted_at_Wat_Chedi_Liem-KayEss-1.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Sermon_in_the_Deer_Park_depicted_at_Wat_Chedi_Liem-KayEss-1.jpeg)

Painting of the Buddha's first sermon from [Wat Chedi Liem](/wiki/Wat_Chedi_Liem "Wat Chedi Liem") in Thailand

### Core teachings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Core teachings")]

The core of Theravāda Buddhist doctrine is contained in the Pāli Canon, the only complete collection of [early Buddhist Texts](/wiki/Early_Buddhist_Texts "Early Buddhist Texts") surviving in a classical [Indic language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages").[[99]](#cite_note-101) The fundamentals of Theravadin Buddhism are shared by the other early Buddhist schools and [Mahayana traditions](/wiki/Basic_Points_Unifying_the_Therav%C4%81da_and_the_Mah%C4%81y%C4%81na "Basic Points Unifying the Theravāda and the Mahāyāna"). They include central concepts such as:[[100]](#cite_note-102)

* A doctrine of action ([karma](/wiki/Karma_in_Buddhism "Karma in Buddhism"))—which is based on intention (*[cetanā](/wiki/Cetan%C4%81 "Cetanā")*)—and a related doctrine of [rebirth](/wiki/Rebirth_(Buddhism) "Rebirth (Buddhism)"), which holds that after death, sentient (albeit incompletely awakened) beings will transmigrate to another body, possibly in another [realm](/wiki/Ten_realms "Ten realms"). The type of realm a being is reborn in is determined by the being's past karma, defining a fundamental reality driven by a cycle of birth and death ([saṃsāra](/wiki/Sa%E1%B9%83s%C4%81ra "Saṃsāra")).
* A rejection of other doctrines and practices found in [Brahmanical](/wiki/Brahmanical "Brahmanical") religions like [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), including the notion that the [Vedas](/wiki/Vedas "Vedas") are a divine authority. Any form of sacrifices to the gods (including [animal sacrifices](/wiki/Animal_sacrifice_in_Hinduism "Animal sacrifice in Hinduism")) and [ritual purification](/wiki/Ritual_purification "Ritual purification") by bathing are considered useless and spiritually corrupted.[[101]](#cite_note-103) The Pāli texts also reject the idea that [castes](/wiki/Caste_system_in_India "Caste system in India") are divinely ordained.
* A set of major teachings called the *[bodhipakkhiyādhammā](/wiki/Bodhipakkhiy%C4%81dhamm%C4%81 "Bodhipakkhiyādhammā")* (factors conducive to awakening).
* Descriptions of various meditative practices or states, namely the four [*jhanas*](/wiki/Dhy%C4%81na_in_Buddhism "Dhyāna in Buddhism") (meditative absorptions) and the formless dimensions (*arupāyatana*).
* [Ethical training](/wiki/Buddhist_ethics "Buddhist ethics") (*sila*), including the ten courses of wholesome action and the [five precepts](/wiki/Five_precepts "Five precepts").
* [Nirvana](/wiki/Nirvana "Nirvana") (Pali: *nibbana*), the highest good and final state in Theravāda Buddhism. It is the complete and final end of suffering, a state of perfection. It is also the end of all rebirth, but it is *not* an annihilation (*[uccheda](/wiki/Ucchedavada "Ucchedavada")*).[[102]](#cite_note-104)
* The corruptions or influxes (*[asava](/wiki/Asava "Asava")*s), such as the corruption of sensual pleasures (*kāmāsava*), existence corruption (*bhavāsava*), and ignorance corruption (*avijjāsava*).
* The doctrine of impermanence (*[anicca](/wiki/Impermanence "Impermanence")*), which holds that all physical and mental phenomena are transient, unstable, and inconstant.[[103]](#cite_note-DavidsStede1921p355-105)
* The doctrine of not-self (*[Anattā](/wiki/Anatt%C4%81 "Anattā")*), which holds that all the constituents of a person, namely, the [five aggregates](/wiki/Skandha "Skandha") ([physical form](/wiki/Rupa_(Buddhism) "Rupa (Buddhism)"), [feelings](/wiki/Vedan%C4%81 "Vedanā"), [perceptions](/wiki/Sa%E1%B9%83j%C3%B1%C4%81 "Saṃjñā"), [intentions](/wiki/Sa%E1%B9%85kh%C4%81ra "Saṅkhāra") and [consciousness](/wiki/Vij%C3%B1%C4%81na "Vijñāna")), are empty of a self (*[atta](/wiki/%C4%80tman_(Hinduism) "Ātman (Hinduism)")*), since they are impermanent and not always under our control. Therefore, there is no unchanging substance, permanent self, [soul](/wiki/Soul "Soul"), or essence.[[104]](#cite_note-106)[[105]](#cite_note-107)
* The [five hindrances](/wiki/Five_hindrances "Five hindrances") (*pañca nīvaraṇāni*), which are obstacles to meditation: (1) sense-desire, (2) hostility, (3) sloth and torpor, (4) restlessness and worry, and (5) doubt.
* The [Four Divine Abodes](/wiki/Brahmavihara "Brahmavihara") (*brahmavihārā*), also known as the four immeasurables (*appamaññā*).
* The [Four Noble Truths](/wiki/Four_Noble_Truths "Four Noble Truths"), which state, in brief: (1) there is [*dukkha*](/wiki/Du%E1%B8%A5kha "Duḥkha") (suffering, unease); (2) there is a cause of *dukkha*, mainly craving (*[taṇhā](/wiki/Ta%E1%B9%87h%C4%81 "Taṇhā")*); (3) the removal of craving leads to the end (*[nirodha](/wiki/Nirodha "Nirodha")*) of suffering; and (4) there is a path (*magga*) to follow to bring this about.[[106]](#cite_note-108)
* The framework of [dependent arising](/wiki/Prat%C4%ABtyasamutp%C4%81da "Pratītyasamutpāda") (*paṭiccasamuppāda*), which explains how suffering arises (beginning with [ignorance](/wiki/Avidy%C4%81_(Buddhism) "Avidyā (Buddhism)") and ending in birth, old age, and death) and how suffering can be brought to an end.[[107]](#cite_note-109)
* The [Middle Way](/wiki/Middle_Way "Middle Way"), which is seen as having two major facets. First, it is a middle path between extreme asceticism and sensual indulgence. It is also seen as a middle view between the idea that at death beings are annihilated and that there is an eternal self (Pali: *atta*).
* The [Noble Eightfold Path](/wiki/Noble_Eightfold_Path "Noble Eightfold Path"), one of the main outlines of the [Buddhist paths to liberation](/wiki/Buddhist_paths_to_liberation "Buddhist paths to liberation"). The eight factors are: Right View, Right Intention, Right Speech, Right Conduct, Right Livelihood, Right Effort, Right Mindfulness, and Right *[Samadhi](/wiki/Samadhi "Samadhi")*.
* The practice of taking [refuge](/wiki/Refuge_(Buddhism) "Refuge (Buddhism)") in the Triple Gems: the [Buddha](/wiki/Gautama_Buddha "Gautama Buddha"), the [Dhamma](/wiki/Dharma_(Buddhism) "Dharma (Buddhism)"), and the [Saṅgha](/wiki/Sangha_(Buddhism) "Sangha (Buddhism)").
* The [Seven Factors of Awakening](/wiki/Seven_Factors_of_Awakening "Seven Factors of Awakening") (*satta bojjhaṅgā*): mindfulness (*[sati](/wiki/Mindfulness_(Buddhism) "Mindfulness (Buddhism)")*), investigation (*[dhamma vicaya](/wiki/Dhamma_vicaya "Dhamma vicaya")*), energy (*[viriya](/wiki/V%C4%ABrya "Vīrya")*), bliss (*[pīti](/wiki/P%C4%ABti "Pīti")*)*,* relaxation (*[passaddhi](/wiki/Passaddhi "Passaddhi")*), [samādhi](/wiki/Sam%C4%81dhi_(Buddhism) "Samādhi (Buddhism)"), and equanimity (*[upekkha](/wiki/Upekkha "Upekkha")*).
* The [six sense bases (*saḷāyatana*)](/wiki/%C4%80yatana "Āyatana") and a corresponding theory of [sense](/wiki/Sense "Sense") impression ([*phassa*](/wiki/Spar%C5%9Ba "Sparśa")) and consciousness ([*viññana*](/wiki/Vij%C3%B1%C4%81na "Vijñāna")).[[108]](#cite_note-110)
* Various frameworks for the practice of [mindfulness](/wiki/Sati_(Buddhism) "Sati (Buddhism)") (*sati*)—mainly, the four *[satipatthana](/wiki/Satipatthana "Satipatthana")*s (establishments of mindfulness) and the 16 elements of [*anapanasati*](/wiki/%C4%80n%C4%81p%C4%81nasati_Sutta "Ānāpānasati Sutta") (mindfulness of breathing).

### Main doctrinal differences with other Buddhist traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Main doctrinal differences with other Buddhist traditions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Moggallana%CC%9E-statue.jpg/250px-Moggallana%CC%9E-statue.jpg)](/wiki/File:Moggallana%CC%9E-statue.jpg)

A statue of the [arahant](/wiki/Arahant "Arahant") [Moggallana](/wiki/Maudgalyayana "Maudgalyayana"), who is identifiable by his dark (*nila*, i.e. blue/black) skin. He was one of the two most senior disciples of the Buddha and regarded as[*[according to whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] the foremost in psychic powers.

The orthodox standpoints of Theravāda in comparison to other [Buddhist denominations](/wiki/Buddhist_denominations "Buddhist denominations") are presented in the *[Kathavatthu](/wiki/Kathavatthu "Kathavatthu")* (Points of Controversy), as well as in other works by commentators like [Buddhaghosa](/wiki/Buddhaghosa "Buddhaghosa").

Traditionally, Theravāda maintains the following key doctrinal positions, though not all Theravādins agree with the traditional points of view:[[109]](#cite_note-111)[[110]](#cite_note-112)

* On the [philosophy of time](/wiki/Philosophy_of_time "Philosophy of time"), the Theravāda tradition follows [philosophical presentism](/wiki/Philosophical_presentism "Philosophical presentism")—the view that only present moment phenomena (*dhamma*) exist—rather than the [eternalist](/wiki/Eternalism_(philosophy_of_time) "Eternalism (philosophy of time)") view of the [Sarvāstivādin](/wiki/Sarv%C4%81stiv%C4%81din "Sarvāstivādin") tradition, which held that *dhamma*s exist in all three times—past, present, and future.
* The *arahant* is never a layperson, for they have abandoned the fetters of a layperson, including married life, using money, etc.
* The power (*bala*) of a Buddha is unique and not common to the disciples (*savaka*) or *arahant*s.
* [Theravāda Abhidhamma](/wiki/Therav%C4%81da_Abhidhamma "Theravāda Abhidhamma") holds that a single thought (*citta*) cannot last as long as a day.
* Theravāda Abhidhamma holds that insight into the Four Noble Truths happens instantaneously (*khaṇa*), rather than gradually (*anupubba*), as was held by [Sarvastivada](/wiki/Sarvastivada "Sarvastivada"). The defilements (*[kilesa](/wiki/Kleshas_(Buddhism) "Kleshas (Buddhism)")*) are also abandoned instantaneously, not gradually.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* Theravāda Abhidhamma traditionally rejects the view that there is an intermediate or transitional state ([*antarabhāva*](/wiki/Bardo "Bardo")) between rebirths, instead holding that rebirth happens instantaneously.[[111]](#cite_note-113) However, as has been noted by various modern scholars (e.g., [Bhikkhu Sujato](/wiki/Bhante_Sujato "Bhante Sujato")), there are canonical passages that support the idea of an intermediate state (such as the *Kutuhalasāla Sutta*).[[112]](#cite_note-:0y-114) Some Theravāda scholars (such as [Balangoda Ananda Maitreya Thero](/wiki/Balangoda_Ananda_Maitreya_Thero "Balangoda Ananda Maitreya Thero")) have defended the idea of an intermediate state. It is also a widespread belief some monks and laypersons in the Theravāda world (where it is commonly referred to as the *[gandhabba](/wiki/Gandharva "Gandharva")* or *antarabhāva*).[[113]](#cite_note-115)
* Theravāda does not accept the Mahayana notion that there are two forms of *nibbana*—an inferior "localized" or "abiding" (*pratiṣṭhita*) nirvana and [a non-abiding (*apratiṣṭhita*) nirvana](/wiki/Nirvana_(Buddhism)#Apratiṣṭhita_nirvāna "Nirvana (Buddhism)"). Such a dual nirvana theory is absent in the *sutta*s.[[114]](#cite_note-116) According to the *Kathāvatthu*, there can be no dividing line separating the unconditioned element, and there is no superiority or inferiority in the unity of *nibbana*.[[115]](#cite_note-117)
* Theravāda exegetical works consider *nibbana* to be a *real* existent instead of a *conceptual* or *nominal* existent (*[prajñapti](/wiki/Praj%C3%B1aptiv%C4%81da "Prajñaptivāda")*), referring to the mere destruction (*khayamatta*) of the defilements or non-existence of the five aggregates, as was held by some in the [Sautrāntika](/wiki/Sautr%C4%81ntika "Sautrāntika") school, for example.[[116]](#cite_note-118) In Theravāda scholasticism, *nibbana* is defined as the cessation (*nirodha*) of non-arising and exists separately from the mere destruction of passion, hatred, and delusion.[[117]](#cite_note-119)
* In Theravāda exegetical works, mental phenomena last briefly or instantly (*khaṇa*), but physical phenomena do not.
* Theravāda holds that the Buddha resided in the human realm (*manussa-loka*). It rejects the [docetic view](/wiki/Docetism "Docetism") found in Mahayana that the Buddha's physical body was an incorporeal manifestation, emanation, or magical creation (*nirmāṇa*) of a transcendental being, and thus, that his birth and death were an illusion.[[118]](#cite_note-120) Also, the Theravāda school rejects the view that there are currently numerous Buddhas in all directions.
* Theravāda holds that there is a ground level of consciousness called the [bhavaṅga](/w/index.php?title=Bhava%E1%B9%85ga&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bhavaṅga (page does not exist)"), which conditions the rebirth consciousness.
* Theravāda rejects the [Pudgalavada](/wiki/Pudgalavada "Pudgalavada") doctrine of the *pudgala* ("person" or "personal entity") as being more than a conceptual designation imputed on the five aggregates.[[119]](#cite_note-Doniger1980p168-121)[[120]](#cite_note-122)
* Theravāda rejects the view of the [Lokottaravāda](/wiki/Lokottarav%C4%81da "Lokottaravāda") schools that all acts done by the [Buddha](/wiki/Gautama_Buddha "Gautama Buddha") (including all speech, defecation, and urination, etc.) were supramundane or transcendental (*lokuttara*).[[121]](#cite_note-123) In Theravāda, a Buddha does not have the power to stop something that has already arisen from ceasing; they cannot stop a being from getting old, sick, or dying, and they cannot create a permanent thing (like a flower that does not die).
* Theravāda traditionally defends the idea that the Buddha himself taught the *Abhidhamma Pitaka*.[[122]](#cite_note-124) This is now being questioned by some modern Theravādins in light of modern Buddhist studies scholarship.
* In Theravāda, [*nibbana*](/wiki/Nirvana_(Buddhism) "Nirvana (Buddhism)") is the only unconstructed phenomenon (*asankhata-dhamma, asankhatadhatu).* Unlike in the [Sarvastivada](/wiki/Sarvastivada "Sarvastivada") school, [space](/wiki/Space "Space") (*[akasa](/wiki/Akasha "Akasha")*), is seen as a constructed *dhamma* in Theravāda. Even the four noble truths are not unconstructed phenomena, nor is the domain of cessation (*nirodhasamapatti*). "Thatness" (*[tathatā](/wiki/Tath%C4%81t%C4%81 "Tathātā")*) is also a constructed phenomenon. According to the *[Dhammasangani](/wiki/Dhammasa%E1%B9%85ga%E1%B9%87%C4%AB "Dhammasaṅgaṇī")*, *nibbana*, the unconstructed element, is 'without condition' (*appaccaya*) and is different from the five aggregates which are 'with condition' (*sappaccaya*).[[123]](#cite_note-125)
* In Theravāda, the [bodhisatta path](/wiki/Bodhisattva "Bodhisattva") is suitable only for a few exceptional people (like Sakyamuni and Metteya).[[124]](#cite_note-126) Theravāda also defines a bodhisatta as someone who has made a vow in front of a living Buddha.[[125]](#cite_note-127)
* In Theravāda, a physical sensory organ (*indriya*) conditions the mental consciousness (*manovinñāna*) and is the material support for consciousness. Some later Theravāda works like the *Visuddhimagga* locate this physical basis for consciousness at the [heart](/wiki/Heart "Heart") (*hadaya-vatthu*), the Pali Canon itself is silent on this issue.[[126]](#cite_note-:12-128)[[127]](#cite_note-:5-129) Some modern Theravāda scholars propose alternative notions. For example, [Suwanda H. J. Sugunasiri](/wiki/Suwanda_H._J._Sugunasiri "Suwanda H. J. Sugunasiri") proposes that the basis for consciousness is the entire physical organism, which he ties with the canonical concept of *[jīvitindriya](/wiki/J%C4%ABvitindriya "Jīvitindriya")* or life faculty.[[126]](#cite_note-:12-128) W. F. Jayasuriya meanwhile, argues that "*hadaya*" is not meant literally (it can also mean "essence", "core"), but refers to the entire [nervous system](/wiki/Nervous_system "Nervous system") (including the brain), which is dependent on the heart and blood.[[127]](#cite_note-:5-129)
* Theravādins generally reject the [Mahayana sutras](/wiki/Mahayana_sutras "Mahayana sutras") as *[Buddhavacana](/wiki/Buddhavacana "Buddhavacana")* (word of the Buddha), and do not study or see these texts (or Mahayana doctrines) as reliable sources. They reject the view that the *Tipitaka* is incomplete or inferior (i.e., "[Hinayana](/wiki/Hinayana "Hinayana")") and that Mahayana texts are somehow more advanced.[[128]](#cite_note-:1-130)
* Theravādins traditionally believe that an awakened [arahant](/wiki/Arhat "Arhat") has an "incorruptible nature" and is thus morally perfect.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarder2000283-131) They have no ignorance or doubts. According to Theravāda doctrine, arahants (and the other three lesser ariyas: stream enterers, etc.) cannot fall back or regress from their state.[[130]](#cite_note-132)
