[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Ledi_Sayadaw_portrait.jpg/250px-Ledi_Sayadaw_portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ledi_Sayadaw_portrait.jpg)

[Ledi Sayadaw](/wiki/Ledi_Sayadaw "Ledi Sayadaw") was one of the great Abhidhamma scholars of the 20th century as well as a teacher of meditation.

Theravāda scholastics developed a systematic exposition of the Buddhist doctrine called the [Abhidhamma](/wiki/Abhidharma "Abhidharma"). In the Pāli Nikayas, the Buddha teaches through an analytical method in which experience is explained using various conceptual groupings of physical and mental processes called "dhammas". Examples of lists of dhammas taught by the Buddha include the twelve sense 'spheres' or [ayatanas](/wiki/Ayatana "Ayatana"), the [five aggregates](/wiki/Skandha "Skandha") or khandha, and the eighteen elements of cognition or [dhatus](/wiki/Skandha#Eighteen_Dhātus_and_Four_Paramatthas "Skandha").[[131]](#cite_note-133)

Theravāda traditionally promotes itself as the [*Vibhajjavāda*](/wiki/Vibhajyav%C4%81da "Vibhajyavāda") "teaching of analysis" and as the heirs to the Buddha's analytical method. Expanding this model, Theravāda Abhidhamma scholasticism concerned itself with analyzing "[ultimate truth](/wiki/Two_truths_doctrine "Two truths doctrine")" (*paramattha-sacca*), which it sees as being composed of *all possible* dhammas and their relationships. The central theory of the Abhidhamma is thus known as the "[dhamma theory](/wiki/Dhamma_theory "Dhamma theory")".[[132]](#cite_note-Karunadasa-134)[[133]](#cite_note-135) "Dhamma" has been translated as "factors" (Collett Cox), "psychic characteristics" (Bronkhorst), "psycho-physical events" (Noa Ronkin), and "phenomena" ([Nyanaponika Thera](/wiki/Nyanaponika_Thera "Nyanaponika Thera")).[[134]](#cite_note-136)[[6]](#cite_note-Crosby,_Kate_2013_p._2-8)

According to the Sri Lankan scholar [Y. Karunadasa](/wiki/Y._Karunadasa "Y. Karunadasa"), a dhammas ("principles" or "elements") are "those items that result when the process of analysis is taken to its ultimate limits".[[132]](#cite_note-Karunadasa-134) However, this does not mean that they have an independent existence, for it is "only for the purposes of description" that they are postulated.[[135]](#cite_note-Karunadasa,_Y_p._14-137) Noa Ronkin defines dhammas as "the constituents of sentient experience; the irreducible 'building blocks' that make up one's world, albeit they are not static mental contents and certainly not substances."[[136]](#cite_note-138) Thus, while in Theravāda Abhidhamma, dhammas are the ultimate constituents of experience, they are not seen as [substances](/wiki/Substance_theory "Substance theory"), [essences](/wiki/Essence "Essence") or independent particulars, since they are empty ([*suñña*](/wiki/%C5%9A%C5%ABnyat%C4%81 "Śūnyatā")) of a self ([*attā*](/wiki/%C4%80tman_(Hinduism) "Ātman (Hinduism)")) and conditioned.[[137]](#cite_note-139) This is spelled out in the [*Patisambhidhamagga*](/wiki/Patisambhidamagga "Patisambhidamagga"), which states that dhammas are empty of [svabhava](/wiki/Svabhava "Svabhava") (*sabhavena suññam*).[[138]](#cite_note-140)

According to Ronkin, the canonical Pāli Abhidhamma remains pragmatic and psychological, and "does not take much interest in [ontology](/wiki/Ontology "Ontology")" in contrast with the [Sarvastivada](/wiki/Sarvastivada "Sarvastivada") tradition. Paul Williams also notes that the Abhidhamma remains focused on the practicalities of insight meditation and leaves ontology "relatively unexplored".[[139]](#cite_note-141) Ronkin does note, however, that later Theravāda sub-commentaries (*ṭīkā*) do show a doctrinal shift towards ontological [realism](/wiki/Philosophical_realism "Philosophical realism") from the earlier epistemic and practical concerns.[[140]](#cite_note-142)

On the other hand, Y. Karunadasa contends that the tradition of realism goes back to the earliest discourses, as opposed to developing only in later Theravada sub-commentaries:

> If we base ourselves on the Pali Nikayas, then we should be compelled to conclude that Buddhism is realistic. There is no explicit denial anywhere of the external world. Nor is there any positive evidence to show that the world is mind-made or simply a projection of subjective thoughts. That Buddhism recognizes the extra-mental existence of matter and, the external world is clearly suggested by the texts. Throughout the discourses it is the language of realism that one encounters. The whole Buddhist practical doctrine and discipline, which has the attainment of Nibbana as its final goal, is based on the recognition of the material world and the conscious living beings living therein.[[141]](#cite_note-143)

The Theravāda Abhidhamma holds that there is a total of 82 possible types of dhammas, 81 of which are conditioned (*[sankhata](/wiki/Sankhata "Sankhata")*), while one is unconditioned, which is [*nibbana*](/wiki/Nirvana_(Buddhism) "Nirvana (Buddhism)"). The 81 conditioned dhammas are divided into three broad categories: consciousness (*[citta](/wiki/Citta "Citta")*), associated mentality ([*cetasika*](/wiki/Mental_factors_(Buddhism) "Mental factors (Buddhism)")) and materiality, or physical phenomena (*rupa*).[[142]](#cite_note-:4-144) Since no dhamma exists independently, every single dhamma of consciousness, known as a *citta*, arises associated (*sampayutta*) with at least seven [mental factors (*cetasikas*)](/wiki/Mental_factors_(Buddhism) "Mental factors (Buddhism)").[[143]](#cite_note-:3-145) In Abhidhamma, all awareness events are thus seen as being characterized by [intentionality](/wiki/Intentionality "Intentionality") and never exist in isolation.[[142]](#cite_note-:4-144) Much of Abhidhamma philosophy deals with categorizing the different consciousnesses and their accompanying mental factors as well as their conditioned relationships (*paccaya*).[[143]](#cite_note-:3-145)

### Cosmology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Cosmology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/018_Sakka_in_Tavatimsa_Heaven_%2830583616922%29.jpg/250px-018_Sakka_in_Tavatimsa_Heaven_%2830583616922%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:018_Sakka_in_Tavatimsa_Heaven_(30583616922).jpg)

Sakka in Tavatimsa Heaven, Wat Yang Thong, [Songkhla](/wiki/Songkhla "Songkhla"), Thailand

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Ngaye_%28Naraka%29_in_Burmese_art.jpg/250px-Ngaye_%28Naraka%29_in_Burmese_art.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ngaye_(Naraka)_in_Burmese_art.jpg)

A Burmese depiction of a hell scene

Main article: [Buddhist cosmology of the Theravada school](/wiki/Buddhist_cosmology_of_the_Theravada_school "Buddhist cosmology of the Theravada school")

The Pāli Tipiṭaka outlines a hierarchical cosmological system with various [planes of existence](/wiki/Plane_(esotericism) "Plane (esotericism)") (*bhava*) into which sentient beings may be reborn depending on their past actions. Good actions lead one to the higher realms, and bad actions lead to the lower realms.[[144]](#cite_note-:2-146)[[145]](#cite_note-:1t-147) However, even for the gods (*devas*) in the higher realms like [Indra](/wiki/Indra "Indra"), there is still death, loss and suffering.[[146]](#cite_note-148)

The main categories of the planes of existence are:[[144]](#cite_note-:2-146)[[145]](#cite_note-:1t-147)

* ***Arūpa-bhava***, the formless or incorporeal plane. These are associated with the [four formless meditations](/wiki/Dhy%C4%81na_in_Buddhism "Dhyāna in Buddhism"): infinite space, infinite consciousness, infinite nothingness, and neither perception nor non-perception. Beings in these realms live extremely long lives (thousands of [kappas](/wiki/Kalpa_(aeon) "Kalpa (aeon)")).
* ***Kāma-bhava***, the spiritual plane of desires. This includes numerous realms of existence such as: various [hells](/wiki/Hell "Hell") ([*niraya*](/wiki/Naraka_(Buddhism) "Naraka (Buddhism)")) which are devoid of happiness, the realms of animals, the hungry ghosts ([*peta*](/wiki/Preta "Preta")), the realm of humans, and various [heaven](/wiki/Heaven "Heaven") realms where the [devas](/wiki/Deva_(Buddhism) "Deva (Buddhism)") live (such as [Tavatimsa](/wiki/Tr%C4%81yastri%E1%B9%83%C5%9Ba "Trāyastriṃśa") and [Tusita](/wiki/Tushita "Tushita")).
* ***Rūpa-bhava***, the plane of form. The realms in this plane are associated with the four meditative absorptions (*jhanas*), and those who attain these meditations are reborn in these divine realms.

These various planes of existence can be found in countless world systems (*loka-dhatu*), which are born, expand, contract, and are destroyed in a cyclical nature across vast expanses of time (measures in kappas). This cosmology is similar to other ancient Indian systems, such as the [Jain cosmology](/wiki/Jain_cosmology "Jain cosmology").[[145]](#cite_note-:1t-147) This entire cyclical multiverse of constant birth and death is called [samsara](/wiki/Sa%E1%B9%83s%C4%81ra "Saṃsāra"). Outside of this system of samsara is *[nibbana](/wiki/Nirvana_(Buddhism) "Nirvana (Buddhism)")* (lit. 'vanishing, quenching, blowing out'), a deathless (*amata*) and [transcendent](/wiki/Transcendence_(religion) "Transcendence (religion)") reality, which is a total and final release (*[vimutti](/wiki/Moksha "Moksha")*) from all suffering (*dukkha*) and rebirth.[[147]](#cite_note-149)

### Soteriology and Buddhology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Soteriology and Buddhology")]

According to Theravāda doctrine, release from suffering (i.e. *nibbana*) is attained in [four stages](/wiki/Four_stages_of_enlightenment "Four stages of enlightenment") of awakening (*[bodhi](/wiki/Enlightenment_in_Buddhism "Enlightenment in Buddhism")*):[[web 2]](#cite_note-150)[[web 3]](#cite_note-supramundane-151)

1. *[Stream-Enterers](/wiki/Sotapanna "Sotapanna")*: Those who have destroyed the first three [fetters](/wiki/Fetter_(Buddhism) "Fetter (Buddhism)") (the false [view](/wiki/View_(Buddhism) "View (Buddhism)") of self, doubt/indecision, and clinging to ethics and vows);[[web 4]](#cite_note-152)[[web 5]](#cite_note-153)
2. *[Once-Returners](/wiki/Sakadagami "Sakadagami")*: Those who have destroyed the first three fetters and have weakened the fetters of desire and ill-will;
3. *[Non-Returners](/wiki/Anagami "Anagami")*: Those who have destroyed the five lower fetters, which bind beings to the world of the senses;[[148]](#cite_note-Shaw_8-154)
4. *[Arahants](/wiki/Arahants "Arahants")* (lit. "honorable" or "worthies"): Those who have realized *[Nibbana](/wiki/Nirvana_(Buddhism) "Nirvana (Buddhism)")* and are free from all defilements. They have abandoned all ignorance, craving for existence, restlessness (*[uddhacca](/wiki/Uddhacca "Uddhacca")*) and subtle pride (*[māna](/wiki/M%C4%81na "Māna")*).[[148]](#cite_note-Shaw_8-154)

In Theravāda Buddhism, a [Buddha](/wiki/Buddhahood "Buddhahood") is a sentient being who has discovered the path out of samsara by themselves, has reached Nibbana, and then makes the path available to others by teaching (known as "turning the wheel of the Dhamma"). A Buddha is also believed to have extraordinary powers and abilities ([*abhiññā*](/wiki/Abhij%C3%B1%C4%81 "Abhijñā")), such as the ability to read minds and fly through the air.[[149]](#cite_note-155)

The Theravāda canon depicts [Gautama Buddha](/wiki/Gautama_Buddha "Gautama Buddha") as the most recent Buddha in a line of previous Buddhas stretching back for aeons. They also mention the future Buddha, named [Metteyya](/wiki/Maitreya "Maitreya").[[150]](#cite_note-156) Traditionally, the Theravāda school also rejects the idea that there can be numerous Buddhas active in the world simultaneously.[[151]](#cite_note-157)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Sumedha_and_D%C4%ABpankara_Buddha%2C_Burmese_manuscript.png/250px-Sumedha_and_D%C4%ABpankara_Buddha%2C_Burmese_manuscript.png)](/wiki/File:Sumedha_and_D%C4%ABpankara_Buddha,_Burmese_manuscript.png)

A Burmese illustrated manuscript depicting Sumedha (the future Buddha Gautama) and [Dīpankara](/wiki/D%C4%ABpankara_Buddha "Dīpankara Buddha") Buddha

Regarding how a sentient being becomes a Buddha, the Theravāda school also presents this path. Indeed, according to [Buddhaghosa](/wiki/Buddhaghosa "Buddhaghosa"), there are three main soteriological paths: the path of the Buddhas (*buddhayāna*); the way of the individual Buddhas (*paccekabuddhayāna*); and the way of the disciples (*sāvakayāna*).[[152]](#cite_note-158)

However, unlike in Mahayana Buddhism, the Theravāda holds that the Buddha path is not for everyone and that beings on the Buddha path ([*bodhisattas*](/wiki/Bodhisattva "Bodhisattva")) are quite rare.[[153]](#cite_note-159) While in Mahayana, bodhisattas refers to beings who have developed the [wish to become Buddhas](/wiki/Bodhicitta "Bodhicitta"), Theravāda (like other early Buddhist schools), defines a bodhisatta as someone who has resolved (*abhinīhāra*) to become a Buddha in front of a living Buddha, and has also received a confirmation from that Buddha that they will reach Buddhahood.[[154]](#cite_note-:02-160) *[Cariyāpiṭaka](/wiki/Cariyapitaka "Cariyapitaka")* is a Theravāda text which focuses on the path of the Buddhas, while the [*Nidānakathā*](/wiki/Jataka_tales "Jataka tales") and the *[Buddhavaṃsa](/wiki/Buddhavamsa "Buddhavamsa")* are also Theravāda texts which discuss the Buddha's path.[[154]](#cite_note-:02-160)

### Modern developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Modern developments")]

The modern era saw new developments in Theravāda scholarship due to the influence of Western thought. As Donald K. Swearer writes:

> Although monastic education is still grounded in the study of Buddhist texts, doctrine, and the Pali language, the curricula of monastic colleges and universities also reflect subject matter and disciplines associated with Western education.[[155]](#cite_note-Swearer,_Donald_K_p._1642-161)

[Buddhist modernist](/wiki/Buddhist_modernism "Buddhist modernism") trends can be traced to figures like [Anagarika Dhammapala](/wiki/Anagarika_Dharmapala "Anagarika Dharmapala"), King [Mongkut](/wiki/Mongkut "Mongkut"), and the first prime minister of Burma [U Nu](/wiki/U_Nu "U Nu").[[156]](#cite_note-162) They promoted a form of Buddhism that was compatible with [rationalism](/wiki/Rationalism "Rationalism") and science, and opposed to superstition and certain folk practices. [Walpola Rahula](/wiki/Walpola_Rahula_Thero "Walpola Rahula Thero")'s *[What the Buddha Taught](/wiki/What_the_Buddha_Taught "What the Buddha Taught")* is seen by scholars as an introduction to modernist Buddhist thought, and the book continues to be widely used in universities.[[155]](#cite_note-Swearer,_Donald_K_p._1642-161)

Another modern phenomenon is Buddhist philosophers who received an education in the West, such as [K. N. Jayatilleke](/wiki/K._N._Jayatilleke "K. N. Jayatilleke") (a student of [Wittgenstein](/wiki/Ludwig_Wittgenstein "Ludwig Wittgenstein") at [Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge")) and [Hammalawa Saddhatissa](/wiki/Hammalawa_Saddhatissa "Hammalawa Saddhatissa") (who received his Phd at [Edinburgh](/wiki/University_of_Edinburgh "University of Edinburgh")), going on to write modern works on [Buddhist philosophy](/wiki/Buddhist_philosophy "Buddhist philosophy") (*Early Buddhist Theory of Knowledge*, 1963 and *Buddhist Ethics*, 1987 respectively). [Henepola Gunaratana](/wiki/Henepola_Gunaratana "Henepola Gunaratana") is another modern Theravāda scholar who studied philosophy in the West (at the [American University](/wiki/American_University "American University")). The modern encounter with Christian missionaries also led to new debates (such as the [Panadura debate](/wiki/Migettuwatte_Gunananda_Thera#Panadura_Debate_පානදුරාවාදය "Migettuwatte Gunananda Thera")) and doctrinal works written in defense of Buddhism or attacking Christian ideas, such as Gunapala Dharmasiri's *A Buddhist critique of the Christian concept of God* (1988).

There have also been several modern Theravāda scholars who have taken a historical-critical perspective on Theravāda literature and doctrine, attempting to understand its historical development. Some of these figures, such as [David Kalupahana](/wiki/David_Kalupahana "David Kalupahana"), [Buddhadasa](/wiki/Buddhadasa "Buddhadasa"), and [Bhikkhu Sujato](/wiki/Bhante_Sujato "Bhante Sujato"), have criticized traditional Theravāda commentators like Buddhaghosa for their doctrinal innovations which differ in significant ways from the early Buddhist texts.[[157]](#cite_note-163)[[158]](#cite_note-164)[[159]](#cite_note-165)

The modern era also saw new Buddhist works on topics which pre-modern Buddhists avoided, such as socially [engaged Buddhism](/wiki/Engaged_Buddhism "Engaged Buddhism") and [Buddhist economics](/wiki/Buddhist_economics "Buddhist economics"). Thinkers such as Buddhadasa, [Sulak Sivaraksa](/wiki/Sulak_Sivaraksa "Sulak Sivaraksa"), [Prayudh Payutto](/wiki/Prayudh_Payutto "Prayudh Payutto"), [Neville Karunatilake](/wiki/Neville_Karunatilake "Neville Karunatilake"), and Padmasiri de Silva have written on these topics. Modern scholarship in Western languages by Western Buddhist monks such as [Nyanatiloka](/wiki/Nyanatiloka "Nyanatiloka"), [Nyanaponika](/wiki/Nyanaponika_Thera "Nyanaponika Thera"), [Nyanamoli](/wiki/%C3%91%C4%81%E1%B9%87amoli_Bhikkhu "Ñāṇamoli Bhikkhu"), [Bhikkhu Bodhi](/wiki/Bhikkhu_Bodhi "Bhikkhu Bodhi"), and [Analayo](/wiki/Bhikkhu_Analayo "Bhikkhu Analayo") is another recent development in the Theravāda world.

## Practice (*paṭipatti*)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Practice (paṭipatti)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Dharmachakra%2C_withprint_%28en%29.svg/330px-Dharmachakra%2C_withprint_%28en%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Dharmachakra,_withprint_(en).svg)

The [Dhamma Wheel](/wiki/Dharmachakra "Dharmachakra") with eight spokes usually symbolizes the [Noble Eightfold Path](/wiki/Noble_Eightfold_Path "Noble Eightfold Path").

### Textual basis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Textual basis")]

In the [Pāli Canon](/wiki/P%C4%81li_Canon "Pāli Canon"), the path (*magga*) or way (*patipada*) of Buddhist practice is [described in various ways](/wiki/Buddhist_paths_to_liberation "Buddhist paths to liberation"), one of the most widely used frameworks in Theravāda is the [Noble Eightfold Path](/wiki/Noble_Eightfold_Path "Noble Eightfold Path"):

> The Blessed One said, "Now what, monks, is the Noble Eightfold Path? Right view, right resolve, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration."[[web 6]](#cite_note-166)

The Noble Eightfold Path can also be summarized as the Three Noble Disciplines of [sīla](/wiki/%C5%9A%C4%ABla "Śīla") (moral conduct or discipline), [Samādhi](/wiki/Samadhi "Samadhi") (meditation or concentration) and [Paññā](/wiki/Praj%C3%B1%C4%81_(Buddhism) "Prajñā (Buddhism)") (understanding or wisdom).[[web 7]](#cite_note-167)[[160]](#cite_note-168)[[web 8]](#cite_note-169)

Theravāda orthodoxy takes the [seven stages of purification](/wiki/Visuddhimagga#Seven_Stages_of_Purification "Visuddhimagga") as outlined in the *[Visuddhimagga](/wiki/Visuddhimagga "Visuddhimagga")* as the basic outline of the path to be followed. The *[Visuddhimagga](/wiki/Visuddhimagga "Visuddhimagga")*, a Sinhala Theravāda doctrinal *[summa](/wiki/Summa "Summa")* written in the fifth century by [Buddhaghosa](/wiki/Buddhaghosa "Buddhaghosa"), became the orthodox account of the Theravāda path to liberation in Sri Lanka after the 12th century and this influence spread to other Theravāda nations.[[92]](#cite_note-Crosby,_2013,_p._86-94) It gives the sequence of seven purifications, in three sections:

* The first section (part 1) explains the rules of discipline, and the method for finding a correct temple to practice, or how to meet a [good teacher](/wiki/Kaly%C4%81%E1%B9%87a-mittat%C4%81 "Kalyāṇa-mittatā").
* The second section (part 2) describes [Samatha](/wiki/Samatha "Samatha") (calming) practice, object by object (see [Kammaṭṭhāna](/wiki/Kamma%E1%B9%AD%E1%B9%ADh%C4%81na "Kammaṭṭhāna") for the list of the forty traditional objects). It mentions different stages of [Samādhi](/wiki/Samadhi "Samadhi").
* The third section (parts 3–7) is a description of the five [khandhas](/wiki/Skandha "Skandha"), [ayatanas](/wiki/Ayatana "Ayatana"), the [Four Noble Truths](/wiki/Four_Noble_Truths "Four Noble Truths"), [dependent origination](/wiki/Prat%C4%ABtyasamutp%C4%81da "Pratītyasamutpāda"), and the practise of [Vipassanā](/wiki/Vipassan%C4%81 "Vipassanā") (insight) through the development of wisdom. It emphasizes [different forms of knowledge](/wiki/Vipassan%C4%81-%C3%B1%C4%81%E1%B9%87a "Vipassanā-ñāṇa") emerging because of the practice. This part shows a great analytical effort specific to [Buddhist philosophy](/wiki/Buddhist_philosophy "Buddhist philosophy").

This basic outline is based on the threefold discipline. The emphasis is on understanding the [three marks of existence](/wiki/Three_marks_of_existence "Three marks of existence"), which removes [ignorance](/wiki/Avijja "Avijja"). Understanding destroys the ten [fetters](/wiki/Fetter_(Buddhism) "Fetter (Buddhism)") and leads to [Nibbana](/wiki/Nirvana_(Buddhism) "Nirvana (Buddhism)").

Theravādins believe that every individual is personally responsible for achieving their own self-awakening and liberation, each being responsible for their own [karma](/wiki/Karma_in_Buddhism "Karma in Buddhism") (actions and consequences). Applying knowledge acquired through direct experience and personal realization is more heavily emphasized than beliefs about the nature of reality as revealed by the Buddha.

### Moral conduct

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Moral conduct")]

Giving (Dana) is an important Buddhist virtue. The community of monastics is seen as the most meritorious field of karmic fruitfulness.

[Sīla](/wiki/Buddhist_ethics "Buddhist ethics"), meaning moral conduct, is mainly defined as [right speech](/wiki/Right_speech "Right speech"), [right action](/wiki/Noble_Eightfold_Path#Right_action "Noble Eightfold Path"), and [right livelihood](/wiki/Right_livelihood "Right livelihood"). It is primarily understood through the doctrine of [kamma](/wiki/Karma_in_Buddhism "Karma in Buddhism"). In Theravāda, one's previous intentional actions strongly influence one's present experience. Whatever intended actions are carried out will have future consequences, whether in this life or subsequent lives.[[161]](#cite_note-170) Intention is central to the idea of kamma. Actions done with good intentions, even if they have bad results, will not have negative kammic consequences.

Several sets of precepts or moral trainings (*sikkhāpada*) guide right action. After taking [Refuge](/wiki/Refuge_(Buddhism) "Refuge (Buddhism)") in the Triple Gems, lay Theravādin Buddhists traditionally take the [Five precepts](/wiki/Five_precepts "Five precepts") (whether for life or for a limited time) in the presence of Sangha.[[162]](#cite_note-171) Laypeople also sometimes take an extended set of [Eight precepts](/wiki/Eight_precepts "Eight precepts"), which includes chastity during sacred days of observance such as [Uposatha](/wiki/Uposatha "Uposatha").

Performing good deeds is another important feature of Theravādin Buddhist ethics. Doing so is said to make "merit" ([puñña](/wiki/Merit_(Buddhism) "Merit (Buddhism)")), which results in a better rebirth. The "ten wholesome actions" is a common list of good deeds:[[163]](#cite_note-172)

1. Generosity (Dāna); This typically involves providing monks with "the four requisites"; food, clothing, shelter, and medicine; however, giving to charity and the needy is also considered dāna.
2. Moral conduct (Sīla); Keeping the five precepts and generally refraining from doing harm.
3. Meditation (Bhāvanā).
4. Dedication of merit; doing good deeds in the name of someone who has passed away or in the name of all sentient beings.
5. Rejoicing in merit of good deeds done by others, this is common in communal activities.
6. Rendering service to others; looking after others or needy.
7. Honoring others; showing appropriate deference, particularly to the Buddha, Dhamma and Sangha, and to seniors and parents. Usually done by placing the hands together in [Añjali Mudrā](/wiki/A%C3%B1jali_Mudr%C4%81 "Añjali Mudrā"), and sometimes bowing.
8. [Preaching or sharing the Dhamma](/wiki/Dharma_talk "Dharma talk"); the gift of Dhamma is seen as a form of highest gift. (Dhammapada 354)
9. Listening to Dhamma
10. Having right view or Sammādiṭṭhi; mainly the [Four Noble Truths](/wiki/Four_Noble_Truths "Four Noble Truths") and the [three marks of existence](/wiki/Three_marks_of_existence "Three marks of existence").

### Meditation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Meditation")]

Main article: [Buddhist meditation](/wiki/Buddhist_meditation "Buddhist meditation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Buddhist_Man_Meditating.jpg/330px-Buddhist_Man_Meditating.jpg)](/wiki/File:Buddhist_Man_Meditating.jpg)

Theravādin monks meditating in [Bodh Gaya](/wiki/Bodh_Gaya "Bodh Gaya") ([Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar"), India)

Meditation (Pāli: *Bhāvanā,* literally "causing to become" or cultivation) means the positive cultivation of one's mind.

#### Forms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Forms")]

Theravāda Buddhist meditation practice varies considerably in technique and objects.[[164]](#cite_note-173) Currently, there are also various traditions of Theravāda meditation practice, such as the [Burmese Vipassana tradition](/wiki/Vipassana_movement "Vipassana movement"), the [Thai Forest Tradition](/wiki/Thai_Forest_Tradition "Thai Forest Tradition"), the esoteric *[Borān kammaṭṭhāna](/wiki/Tantric_Theravada "Tantric Theravada")* ('ancient practices'), the Burmese [Weikza](/wiki/Weizza "Weizza") tradition, [Dhammakaya meditation](/wiki/Dhammakaya_meditation "Dhammakaya meditation") and the Western [Insight Meditation](/wiki/Insight_Meditation_Society "Insight Meditation Society") movement.

Theravāda Buddhist meditation practices or [Bhavana](/wiki/Bhavana "Bhavana") (mental cultivation) are categorized into two broad categories: [Samatha](/wiki/Samatha "Samatha") bhavana (calming), and [Vipassanā](/wiki/Vipassan%C4%81 "Vipassanā") bhavana (investigation, insight).[[web 9]](#cite_note-174) Originally these referred to effects or qualities of meditation, but after the time of [Buddhaghosa](/wiki/Buddhaghosa "Buddhaghosa"), they also referred to two distinct meditation types or paths (*yāna*).[[165]](#cite_note-175)[[166]](#cite_note-Vajiranāṇa-Mahathera-176)[[web 3]](#cite_note-supramundane-151)

*[Samatha](/wiki/Samatha "Samatha")* ("calm") consists of meditation techniques in which the mind is focused on a single object, thought, or gatha, leading to *[Samādhi](/wiki/Sam%C4%81dhi "Samādhi")'*. In traditional Theravāda it is considered to be the base for *vipassanā* ("insight"). In the Theravāda-tradition, as early as the [Pāli Nikayas](/wiki/Nik%C4%81ya "Nikāya"), the four *[jhānas](/wiki/Dhy%C4%81na_in_Buddhism "Dhyāna in Buddhism")* are regarded as a *samatha*-practice. The eighth and final step of the Eightfold Path, Right Samadhi, is often defined as the four jhanas.[[167]](#cite_note-177) In the [Pāli Nikayas](/wiki/Nik%C4%81ya "Nikāya"), Jhānas are described as preceding the awakening insight of the Buddha, which turned him into an awakened being.[[web 10]](#cite_note-178) Yet the interpretation of *jhana* as single-pointed concentration and calm may be a later re-interpretation in which the original aim of *jhana* was lost.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPolak2011-179)

*Vipassana* ("insight", "clear seeing") refers to practices that aim to develop an inner understanding or knowledge of the nature of phenomena (*dhammas*), especially the characteristics of *[dukkha](/wiki/Dukkha "Dukkha")*, *[anatta](/wiki/Anatta "Anatta")* and *[anicca](/wiki/Anicca "Anicca")*, which are seen as being universally applicable to all constructed phenomena (*sankhata-dhammas*). *Vipassana* is also described as insight into [dependent origination](/wiki/Prat%C4%ABtyasamutp%C4%81da "Pratītyasamutpāda"), the [five aggregates](/wiki/Skandha "Skandha"), the [sense spheres](/wiki/Ayatana "Ayatana") and the [Four Noble Truths](/wiki/Four_Noble_Truths "Four Noble Truths").[[169]](#cite_note-:10-180)[[166]](#cite_note-Vajiranāṇa-Mahathera-176) It is the primary focus of the modernist Burmese Vipassana movement. In western countries it is complemented with the [four divine abidings](/wiki/Brahmavihara "Brahmavihara"), the development of loving-kindness and compassion.[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFronsdal1998-181)[[171]](#cite_note-182)

*Vipassana* practice begins with the preparatory stage, the practice of *[sila](/wiki/%C5%9A%C4%ABla "Śīla")*, morality, giving up worldly thoughts and desires.[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson201454–55-183)[[173]](#cite_note-184) The practitioner then engages in *[anapanasati](/wiki/Anapanasati "Anapanasati")*, mindfulness of breathing, which is described in the [Satipatthana Sutta](/wiki/Satipatthana_Sutta "Satipatthana Sutta") as going into the forest and sitting beneath a tree and then simply to watch the breath. If the breath is long, to notice that the breath is long, if the breath is short, to notice that the breath is short.[[174]](#cite_note-185)[[175]](#cite_note-186) In the "New Burmese Method" the practitioner [pays attention](/wiki/Sati_(Buddhism) "Sati (Buddhism)") to any arising mental or physical phenomenon, engaging in [vitaka](/wiki/Vitaka "Vitaka"), noting or naming physical and mental phenomena ("breathing, breathing"), without engaging the phenomenon with conceptual thinking.[[176]](#cite_note-Practical-187)[[177]](#cite_note-188) By noticing the arising of physical and mental phenomena the meditator becomes aware of how sense impressions arise from the contact between the senses and physical and mental phenomena,[[176]](#cite_note-Practical-187) as described in the five *[skandhas](/wiki/Skandhas "Skandhas")* and *[paṭiccasamuppāda](/wiki/Pa%E1%B9%ADiccasamupp%C4%81da "Paṭiccasamuppāda")*. The practitioner also becomes aware of the perpetual changes involved in breathing, and the arising and passing away of mindfulness.[[178]](#cite_note-Art-189) This noticing is accompanied by reflections on [causation](/wiki/Pa%E1%B9%ADiccasamupp%C4%81da "Paṭiccasamuppāda") and other Buddhist teachings, leading to insight into [dukkha](/wiki/Dukkha "Dukkha"), [anatta](/wiki/Anatta "Anatta"), and [anicca](/wiki/Anicca "Anicca").[[179]](#cite_note-190)[[178]](#cite_note-Art-189) When the three characteristics have been comprehended, reflection subdues and the process of noticing accelerates, noting phenomena in general without necessarily naming them.[[180]](#cite_note-191)[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENyanaponika1998-192)[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGombrich1997133-193)

According to Vajiranāṇa Mahathera, writing from a traditional and text-based point of view, in the Pāli Canon whether one begins the practice by way of samatha or by way of vipassanā is generally seen as depending on one's temperament. According to Vajiranāṇa Mahathera, it is generally held that there are two kinds of individuals. Those of a passionate disposition (or those who enter the path by faith) attain Arahatship through vipassanā preceded by samatha. Those of a skeptical disposition (or those who enter by way of wisdom or the intellect) achieve it through samatha preceded by vipassanā.[[166]](#cite_note-Vajiranāṇa-Mahathera-176)

#### Aims of meditation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Aims of meditation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/f/ff/Portrait_of_Ajahn_Mun.jpg/250px-Portrait_of_Ajahn_Mun.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_Ajahn_Mun.jpg)

Ajahn Mun, a key figure in the founding of the [Thai Forest Tradition](/wiki/Thai_Forest_Tradition "Thai Forest Tradition"), is widely considered to have been an Arahant in Thailand.[[183]](#cite_note-194)

Traditionally, the ultimate goal of the practice is to achieve mundane and supramundane wisdom. Mundane wisdom is the insight in the [three marks of existence](/wiki/Three_marks_of_existence "Three marks of existence").[[web 3]](#cite_note-supramundane-151) The development of this insight leads to four supramundane paths and fruits, these experiences consist a direct apprehension of Nibbana.[[web 11]](#cite_note-supramundane2-195) Supramundane (*lokuttara)* wisdom refers to that which transcends the world of [samsara](/wiki/Sa%E1%B9%83s%C4%81ra "Saṃsāra").[[web 11]](#cite_note-supramundane2-195)

Apart from [nibbana](/wiki/Nirvana_(Buddhism) "Nirvana (Buddhism)"), there are various reasons why traditional Theravāda Buddhism advocates meditation, including a good [rebirth](/wiki/Rebirth_(Buddhism) "Rebirth (Buddhism)"), [supranormal powers](/wiki/%E1%B9%9Addhi "Ṛddhi"), combating fear and preventing danger. Recent modernist Theravādins have tended to focus on the [psychological benefits](/wiki/Research_on_meditation "Research on meditation") and psychological [well-being](/wiki/Well-being "Well-being").[[184]](#cite_note-196)

#### Historical development and sources

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Historical development and sources")]

The practice of Theravāda meditation can be traced back to the 5th century exegete [Buddhaghosa](/wiki/Buddhaghosa "Buddhaghosa"), who systematized the classic Theravāda meditation, dividing them into samatha and vipassana types and listing [40 different forms](/wiki/Kamma%E1%B9%AD%E1%B9%ADh%C4%81na "Kammaṭṭhāna") (known as "*kammaṭṭhānas*", "workplaces") in his magnum opus, the [Visuddhimagga](/wiki/Visuddhimagga "Visuddhimagga").[[185]](#cite_note-197)[[186]](#cite_note-198) This text has remained central for the study and practice of Theravāda meditation. Buddhaghosa's commentary on the [*Satipatthana sutta*](/wiki/Satipatthana_Sutta "Satipatthana Sutta") ("Bases of mindfulness discourse"), as well as the source text itself, are also another important source for meditation in this tradition.[[187]](#cite_note-crosby2013-199) Buddhaghosa's work drew heavily on the Pali suttas as well as the Pali [Abhidhamma](/wiki/Abhidharma "Abhidharma"). Kate Crosby notes that Buddhaghosa's work also "explicitly refers to the contemporaneous existence of secret meditation manuals but not to their content."[[187]](#cite_note-crosby2013-199)

Regarding post Visuddhimagga Theravāda meditation, according to Kate Crosby,

> In the period between the Visuddhimagga and the present, there have been numerous meditation texts, both manuals and descriptive treatises. Many of the texts found in manuscript collections relate to meditation, some on a single, simple subject such as the recollection of the qualities of the Buddha, others more complex. Little research has been done to assess their variety. One difficulty is that meditation manuals as such are often in a mixture of a classical language, that is, Pali, and a vernacular that may or may not be a currently used language. Also, actual manuals often contain prompts or reminders rather than an in-depth explanation. In recent years it has emerged that there is still extant a relatively high number of manuals and related texts pertaining to a system of meditation called – among other things – borān kammaṭṭhāna or yogāvacara. Its core text, the Mūla-kammaṭṭhāna "original, fundamental or basic meditation practice," circulated under a number of different titles, or without a title, throughout the Tai–Lao–Khmer and Sri Lankan Buddhist worlds. Some versions of this text are simple lists of kammaṭṭhāna and from that perspective look entirely in accord with the Visuddhimagga or Theravada Abhidhamma texts. Other versions contain extensive narratives, explanations of symbolism, and of the somatic locations involved in the practice that make it clear that we are dealing with techniques of practice not described in the Canon or Visuddhimagga.[[187]](#cite_note-crosby2013-199)

According to Crosby, the esoteric borān kammaṭṭhāna or yogāvacara meditation tradition was the dominant form of meditation in the Theravāda world during the 18th century, and may date as far back as the 16th century. Crosby notes that this tradition of meditation involved a rich collection of symbols, somatic methods and visualizations which included "the physical internalisation or manifestation of aspects of the Theravada path by incorporating them at points in the body between the nostril and navel."[[188]](#cite_note-200) In spite of the novel elements in this meditation tradition, close study of borān kammaṭṭhāna texts reveals that they are closely connected to Theravada Abhidhamma and the works of Buddhaghosa.[[189]](#cite_note-201) Modernist reforms which emphasized Pali Canon study, a shift in state support to other traditions and modern wars in Indochina led to this tradition's decline, and it now only survives in a few Cambodian and Thai temples.[[190]](#cite_note-:7-202)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Mahasi_Sayadaw.jpg/250px-Mahasi_Sayadaw.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mahasi_Sayadaw.jpg)

[Mahasi Sayadaw](/wiki/Mahasi_Sayadaw "Mahasi Sayadaw")

During the 19th and 20th centuries, the Theravāda world saw a modernist revival and reinvention of meditation practice, as exemplified by the Burmese Vipassana movement.[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBuswell2004890-203)[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcMahan2008189-204) According to Buswell *vipassana,* "appears to have fallen out of practice" by the 10th century, due to the belief that Buddhism had degenerated, and that liberation was no longer attainable until the coming of *Maitreya*.[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBuswell2004889-205) The practice was revived in Myanmar (Burma) in the 18th century by [Medawi](/wiki/Medawi "Medawi") (1728–1816) and by later figures such as [Ledi Sayadaw](/wiki/Ledi_Sayadaw "Ledi Sayadaw") and [Mahāsī Sayadaw](/wiki/Mahasi_Sayadaw "Mahasi Sayadaw") during the 19th and 20th centuries. These Burmese figures re-invented *vipassana*-meditation and developed simplified meditation techniques, based on the *[Satipatthana sutta](/wiki/Satipatthana_sutta "Satipatthana sutta")*, the *[Visuddhimagga](/wiki/Visuddhimagga "Visuddhimagga")*, and other texts, emphasizing *[satipatthana](/wiki/Satipatthana "Satipatthana")* and bare insight.[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBuswell2004890-203)[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcMahan2008189-204) These techniques were globally popularized by the [Vipassana movement](/wiki/Vipassana_movement "Vipassana movement") in the second half of the 20th century.

Similar revival movements developed in [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), such as the Thai forest tradition and Dhammakaya meditation. These traditions are influenced by the older borān kammaṭṭhāna forms.[[194]](#cite_note-206) Thailand and Cambodia also saw attempts to preserve and revive the ancient "borān kammaṭṭhāna" tradition of meditation.[[195]](#cite_note-207) In Sri Lanka, the new Buddhist traditions of the [Amarapura](/wiki/Amarapura_Nikaya "Amarapura Nikaya") and [Rāmañña Nikāyas](/wiki/Ramanna_Nikaya "Ramanna Nikaya") developed their own meditation forms based on the Pali Suttas, the Visuddhimagga, and other manuals, while borān kammaṭṭhāna mostly disappeared by the end of the 19th century.[[196]](#cite_note-208)

Though the *Vipassana movement* has popularised meditation both in traditional Theravāda countries among the laity, and in western countries, "meditation plays a minor if not negligible role in the lives of the majority of Theravāda monks."[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharf1995242-209)[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrosby2013147-210)[[web 1]](#cite_note-Braun2014-6) Meditation is especially popular laypersons,[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharf1995-211) especially during special religious holidays or in their old age, when they have more free time to spend at the temple.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrosby2013147-210) Buddhist modernists tend to present Buddhism as rational and scientific, and this has also affected how Vipassana meditation has been taught and presented. This has led in some quarters to a playing down of older non-empirical elements of Theravāda, associated with '[superstition](/wiki/Superstition "Superstition")'.[[200]](#cite_note-212) Strains of older, traditional Theravāda meditation known as "borān kammaṭṭhāna" still exist, but this tradition has mostly been eclipsed by the [Buddhist modernist](/wiki/Buddhist_modernism "Buddhist modernism") meditation movements.[[190]](#cite_note-:7-202)

### Other practices

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Other practices")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Walk_with_lighted_candles_in_hand_around_a_temple.jpg/250px-Walk_with_lighted_candles_in_hand_around_a_temple.jpg)](/wiki/File:Walk_with_lighted_candles_in_hand_around_a_temple.jpg)

[Circumambulation](/wiki/Circumambulation "Circumambulation") around a temple or a stupa is also a common devotional practice.

Laypersons and monks also perform various types of religious practices daily or during [Buddhist holidays](/wiki/Buddhist_holidays "Buddhist holidays"). One of these is keeping a [Buddhist shrine](/wiki/Buddhist_shrine "Buddhist shrine") with a picture or statue of the Buddha for devotional practice in one's home, mirroring the larger shrines at temples.[[201]](#cite_note-:6-213) It is common to offer candles, incense, flowers and other objects to these shrine.[[201]](#cite_note-:6-213) Gestures of respect are also done in front of Buddha images and shrines, mainly the respectful salutation with the hands (*añjalikamma*), and the five-limb [prostration](/wiki/Prostration_(Buddhism) "Prostration (Buddhism)") (*pañc'anga-vandana*).[[201]](#cite_note-:6-213)

[Buddhist forms of chanting](/wiki/Buddhist_chant "Buddhist chant") is also widely practiced by both monks and laypersons, who may recite famous phrases such as the taking of [refuge](/wiki/Refuge_(Buddhism) "Refuge (Buddhism)"), the [Metta Sutta](/wiki/Metta_Sutta "Metta Sutta") and the [Mangala Sutta](/wiki/Mangala_Sutta "Mangala Sutta") in front of their shrine. Chanting may also be part of the practice of recollection ([anussati](/wiki/Anussati "Anussati")), which refers to contemplating various topics such as the sublime qualities of [the Buddha](/wiki/The_Buddha "The Buddha"), Dhamma and Sangha or the [five subjects for daily recollection](/wiki/Upajjhatthana_Sutta "Upajjhatthana Sutta").[[201]](#cite_note-:6-213) This may be done as part of a daily [puja](/wiki/Puja_(Buddhism) "Puja (Buddhism)") ritual.

Another important religious practice for the devout is the keeping of special religious holidays known as [Uposatha](/wiki/Uposatha "Uposatha") which are based on a lunar calendar. Laypersons commonly take the eight precepts while visiting a temple or monastery and commit to focusing on Buddhist practice for the day.[[201]](#cite_note-:6-213)

Study (*ganthadhura*) of the Buddhist texts and listening to [Dhamma talks](/wiki/Dharma_talk "Dharma talk") by monks or teachers are also important practices.

## Lay and monastic life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Lay and monastic life")]

See also: [Buddhist monasticism](/wiki/Buddhist_monasticism "Buddhist monasticism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Young_monk.jpg/250px-Young_monk.jpg)](/wiki/File:Young_monk.jpg)

Young Burmese monk

### Distinction between lay and monastic life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Distinction between lay and monastic life")]

Traditionally, Theravāda Buddhism has observed a distinction between the practices suitable for a [lay person](/wiki/Householder_(Buddhism) "Householder (Buddhism)") and the practices undertaken by ordained [monks](/wiki/Bhikkhu "Bhikkhu") (in ancient times, there was a separate body of practices for nuns). While the possibility of significant attainment by laymen is not entirely disregarded by the Theravāda, it generally occupies a position of less prominence than in the [Mahāyāna](/wiki/Mahayana "Mahayana") and [Vajrayāna](/wiki/Vajrayana "Vajrayana") traditions, with monastic life being hailed as a superior method of achieving [Nibbana](/wiki/Nirvana_(Buddhism) "Nirvana (Buddhism)").[[202]](#cite_note-214) The view that Theravāda, unlike other Buddhist schools, is primarily a monastic tradition has, however, been disputed.

> Some Western scholars have erroneously tried to claim that Mahāyāna is primarily a religion for laymen and Theravāda is a primarily monastic religion. Both Mahāyāna and Theravāda have as their foundation strong monastic communities, which are almost identical in their regulations. Schools of Mahāyāna Buddhism without monastic communities of fully ordained monks and nuns are relatively recent and atypical developments, usually based on cultural and historical considerations rather than differences in fundamental doctrine. Both Mahāyāna and Theravāda also provided a clear and important place for lay followers.

— Ron Epstein, "Clearing Up Some Misconceptions about Buddhism"[[203]](#cite_note-215)

[*[dead link](/wiki/Wikipedia:Link_rot "Wikipedia:Link rot")*]

This distinction between ordained monks and laypeople – as well as the distinction between those practices advocated by the Pāli Canon, and the folk religious elements embraced by many monks – have motivated some scholars to consider Theravāda Buddhism to be composed of multiple separate traditions, overlapping though still distinct. Most prominently, the anthropologist [Melford Spiro](/wiki/Melford_Spiro "Melford Spiro") in his work *Buddhism and Society* separated Burmese Theravāda into three groups: Apotropaic Buddhism (concerned with providing protection from evil spirits), Kammatic Buddhism (concerned with making [merit](/wiki/Merit_(Buddhism) "Merit (Buddhism)") for a future birth), and Nibbanic Buddhism (concerned with attaining the liberation of [Nibbana](/wiki/Nirvana_(Buddhism) "Nirvana (Buddhism)"), as described in the Tipitaka). He stresses that all three are firmly rooted in the Pāli Canon. These categories are not accepted by all scholars, and are usually considered non-exclusive by those who employ them.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The role of lay people has traditionally been primarily occupied with activities that are commonly termed *[merit-making](/wiki/Merit_(Buddhism) "Merit (Buddhism)")* (falling under Spiro's category of kammatic Buddhism). Merit-making activities include offering food and other basic necessities to monks, making donations to temples and monasteries, burning incense or lighting candles before images of the Buddha, chanting protective or scriptural verses from the [Pali Canon](/wiki/Pali_Canon "Pali Canon"), building roads and bridges, charity to the needy and providing drinking water to strangers along roadside. Some lay practitioners have always chosen to take a more active role in religious affairs, while still maintaining their lay status. Dedicated lay men and women sometimes act as trustees or custodians for their temples, taking part in the financial planning and management of the temple. Others may volunteer significant time in tending to the mundane needs of local monks (by cooking, cleaning, maintaining temple facilities, etc.). [Study of the Pāli scriptures](/wiki/Pariyatti "Pariyatti") and the practice of [meditation](/wiki/Buddhist_meditation "Buddhist meditation") are less common among the lay community in the past, though in the 20th century these areas have become more popular to the lay community, especially in Thailand.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Monk_on_pilgrimage.jpg/250px-Monk_on_pilgrimage.jpg)](/wiki/File:Monk_on_pilgrimage.jpg)

Thai monks on [pilgrimage](/wiki/Pilgrimage "Pilgrimage") in their orange robes

A number of senior monastics in the Thai Forest Tradition, including [Buddhadasa](/wiki/Buddhadasa "Buddhadasa"), [Ajahn Maha Bua](/wiki/Ajahn_Maha_Bua "Ajahn Maha Bua"), Ajahn Plien Panyapatipo, [Ajahn Pasanno](/wiki/Ajahn_Pasanno "Ajahn Pasanno"), and [Ajahn Jayasaro](/wiki/Ajahn_Jayasaro "Ajahn Jayasaro"), have begun teaching meditation retreats outside of the monastery for lay disciples. [Ajahn Sumedho](/wiki/Ajahn_Sumedho "Ajahn Sumedho"), a disciple of [Ajahn Chah](/wiki/Ajahn_Chah "Ajahn Chah"), founded the [Amaravati Buddhist Monastery](/wiki/Amaravati_Buddhist_Monastery "Amaravati Buddhist Monastery") in Hertfordshire, which has a retreat center specifically for lay retreats. Sumedho extended this to Harnham in Northumberland as [Aruna Ratanagiri](/wiki/Aruna_Ratanagiri "Aruna Ratanagiri") under the present guidance of [Ajahn Munindo](/w/index.php?title=Ajahn_Munindo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ajahn Munindo (page does not exist)"), another disciple of Ajahn Chah.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Lay devotee

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Lay devotee")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Vesak_in_Uttaradit_%28Thailand%29_1.jpg/250px-Vesak_in_Uttaradit_%28Thailand%29_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vesak_in_Uttaradit_(Thailand)_1.jpg)

The ceremony walks with lighted candles in hand around a temple on [Vesakha](/wiki/Vesak "Vesak") Puja in [Uttaradit](/wiki/Uttaradit "Uttaradit"), Thailand.

In Pāli the word for a male lay devotee is [Upasaka](/wiki/Upasaka "Upasaka") and a female devotee is Upasika. One of the duties of the lay followers, as taught by the Buddha, is to look after the needs of the monk/nuns. They are to see that the monk/nuns do not suffer from lack of the four requisites: food, clothing, shelter and medicine. As neither monks nor nuns are allowed to have an occupation, they depend entirely on the laity for their sustenance. In return for this charity, they are expected to lead exemplary lives.

In Myanmar and Thailand, the monastery was and is still regarded as a seat of learning. Theravādin monasteries have been providing free education to many children since ancient times. In fact, today about half of the primary schools in Thailand are located in monasteries. Religious rituals and ceremonies held in a monastery are always accompanied by social activities. In times of crisis, it is to the monks that people bring their problems for counsel and monks often took up the role of mediators in most disputes. Traditionally, a ranking monk will deliver a sermon [four times a month](/wiki/Uposatha "Uposatha"): when the moon waxes and wanes and the day before the new and full moons. The laity also have a chance to learn meditation from the monks during these times.

It is also possible for a lay disciple to become enlightened. As [Bhikkhu Bodhi](/wiki/Bhikkhu_Bodhi "Bhikkhu Bodhi") notes, "The Suttas and commentaries do record a few cases of lay disciples attaining the final goal of Nirvana. However, such disciples either attain Arahantship on the brink of death or enter the monastic order soon after their attainment. They do not continue to dwell at home as Arahant householders, for dwelling at home is incompatible with the state of one who has severed all craving."[[204]](#cite_note-216)

In the modern era, it is now common for lay disciples to practice meditation, attend lay meditation centers and even aim for awakening. The impetus for this trend began in Myanmar and was supported by prime minister [U Nu](/wiki/U_Nu "U Nu") who himself established the International Meditation Center (IMC) in [Yangon](/wiki/Yangon "Yangon").[[205]](#cite_note-:9-217) Modern lay teachers such as [U Ba Khin](/wiki/Ba_Khin "Ba Khin") (who was also the Accountant General of the [Union of Burma](/wiki/Post-independence_Burma,_1948%E2%80%9362 "Post-independence Burma, 1948–62")) promoted meditation as part of a laypersons daily routine.[[205]](#cite_note-:9-217) According to Donald K Swearer, another development in modern Theravāda is "the formation of lay Buddhist associations that have partially assumed the social service responsibilities formerly associated with the monastery".[[205]](#cite_note-:9-217) These include social service and activist organizations such as the [Young Men's Buddhist Association](/wiki/Young_Men%27s_Buddhist_Association "Young Men's Buddhist Association") of Colombo, the All Ceylon Buddhist Congress, the [Sarvodaya Shramadana](/wiki/Sarvodaya_Shramadana_Movement "Sarvodaya Shramadana Movement") of A. T. Ariyaratne, the NGO's founded by [Sulak Sivaraksa](/wiki/Sulak_Sivaraksa "Sulak Sivaraksa") such as Santi Pracha.[[206]](#cite_note-218)

### Monastic vocation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Monastic vocation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Na_uyana_aranya_cave_kuti.jpg/250px-Na_uyana_aranya_cave_kuti.jpg)](/wiki/File:Na_uyana_aranya_cave_kuti.jpg)

A cave *kuti* (hut) in the Sri Lankan forest monastery [Na Uyana Aranya](/wiki/Na_Uyana_Aranya "Na Uyana Aranya")

Theravāda sources dating back to medieval Sri Lanka (2nd century BCE to 10th century CE) such as the [Mahavamsa](/wiki/Mahavamsa "Mahavamsa") show that monastic roles in the tradition were often seen as being in a polarity between urban monks ([Sinhala](/wiki/Sinhala_language "Sinhala language"): *khaamawaasii*, Pāli: *gāmavasī*) on one end and rural forest monks ([Sinhala](/wiki/Sinhala_language "Sinhala language"): *aranyawaasii*, Pali: *araññavasi, nagaravasi*, also known as *Tapassin*) on the other.[[207]](#cite_note-219) The ascetic focused monks were known by the names *Pamsukulikas* (rag robe wearers) and *Araññikas* (forest dwellers).[[208]](#cite_note-220)

The Mahavamsa mentions forest monks associated with the [Mahavihara](/wiki/Mahavihara "Mahavihara"). The Pāli [Dhammapada](/wiki/Dhammapada "Dhammapada") Commentary mentions another split based on the "duty of study" and the "duty of contemplation".[[209]](#cite_note-221) This second division has traditionally been seen as corresponding with the city – forest split, with the city monks focusing on the vocation of books (*ganthadhura*) or learning (*pariyatti*) while the forest monks leaning more towards meditation (*vipassanadhura*) and practice (*patipatti*).[[21]](#cite_note-:0-23) However this opposition is not consistent, and urban monasteries have often promoted meditation while forest communities have also produced excellent scholars, such as the [Island Hermitage](/wiki/Island_Hermitage "Island Hermitage") of [Nyanatiloka](/wiki/Nyanatiloka "Nyanatiloka").[[21]](#cite_note-:0-23)

Scholar monks generally undertake the path of studying and preserving Theravāda's [Pāli literature](/wiki/Pali_literature "Pali literature").[[210]](#cite_note-222) Forest monks tend to be the minority among Theravāda sanghas and also tend to focus on [asceticism](/wiki/Asceticism "Asceticism") (*[dhutanga](/wiki/Dhutanga "Dhutanga")*) and meditative praxis.[[211]](#cite_note-223) They view themselves as living closer to the ideal set forth by the Buddha, and are often perceived as such by lay folk, while at the same time often being on the margins of the Buddhist establishment and on the periphery of the social order.[[212]](#cite_note-224)

While this divide seems to have been in existence for some time in the Theravāda school, only in the 10th century is a specifically forest monk monastery, mentioned as existing near [Anuradhapura](/wiki/Anuradhapura "Anuradhapura"), called "Tapavana".[[213]](#cite_note-225) This division was then carried over into the rest of Southeast Asia as Theravāda spread.

Today there are forest based traditions in most Theravāda countries, including the [Sri Lankan Forest Tradition](/wiki/Sri_Lankan_Forest_Tradition "Sri Lankan Forest Tradition"), the [Thai Forest Tradition](/wiki/Thai_Forest_Tradition "Thai Forest Tradition") as well as lesser known forest based traditions in Burma and Laos, such as the Burmese forest based monasteries (*taw"yar*) of the [Pa Auk Sayadaw](/wiki/Bhaddanta_%C4%80ci%E1%B9%87%E1%B9%87a "Bhaddanta Āciṇṇa").[[214]](#cite_note-226) In Thailand, forest monks are known as *phra thudong* (ascetic wandering monks) or *phra thudong kammathan* (wandering ascetic meditator).[[215]](#cite_note-227)

### Ordination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Ordination")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Candidate_for_the_Buddhist_priesthood_is_ordaining_to_is_a_monk_in_a_church.jpg/250px-Candidate_for_the_Buddhist_priesthood_is_ordaining_to_is_a_monk_in_a_church.jpg)](/wiki/File:Candidate_for_the_Buddhist_priesthood_is_ordaining_to_is_a_monk_in_a_church.jpg)

Candidates for the Buddhist monkhood being ordained as monks in Thailand

The minimum age for ordaining as a Buddhist monk is 20 years, which is calculated from conception. Those under this age can perform traditional ceremonies such as *[shinbyu](/wiki/Shinbyu "Shinbyu"),* in Myanmar, in order to ordain as novices ([sāmaṇera](/wiki/%C5%9Ar%C4%81ma%E1%B9%87era "Śrāmaṇera")). Novices shave their heads, wear the yellow robes, and observe the Ten Precepts. While there is no explicit minimum age for novices prescribed in the scriptures, it is traditionally accepted that boys as young as seven can be accepted for ordination. This tradition echoes the story of the Buddha's son, Rahula, who was allowed to become a novice at the age of seven. Both monks and nuns are expected to adhere to a specific code of discipline, of which monks follow 227 rules, and nuns follow 311.

In most Theravāda countries, it is a common practice for young men to ordain as monks for a fixed period of time. In Thailand and Myanmar, young men typically ordain for the retreat during [Vassa](/wiki/Vassa "Vassa"), the three-month monsoon season, though shorter or longer periods of ordination are not rare. Traditionally, temporary ordination was even more flexible among Laotians. Once they had undergone their initial ordination as young men, Laotian men were permitted to temporarily ordain again at any time, though married men were expected to seek their wife's permission. Throughout Southeast Asia, there is little stigma attached to leaving the monastic life. Monks regularly leave the robes after acquiring an education, or when compelled by family obligations or ill health.

Ordaining as a monk, even for a short period, is seen as having many virtues. In many Southeast Asian cultures, it is seen as a means for a young man to "repay his gratitude" to his parents for their work and effort in raising him, because the merit from his ordination is dedicated for their well-being. Thai men who have ordained as a monk may be seen as more mature and suitable husbands by Thai women, who refer to men who have served as monks with a colloquial term meaning "ripe" to indicate that they are more mature and ready for marriage. Particularly in rural areas, temporary ordination of boys and young men traditionally offered peasant boys an opportunity to receive free education in temple schools with sponsorship and accommodation.

In Sri Lanka, temporary ordination is not practised, and a monk leaving the order is frowned upon but not condemned. The continuing influence of the [caste system in Sri Lanka](/wiki/Caste_system_in_Sri_Lanka "Caste system in Sri Lanka") plays a role in the taboo against temporary or permanent ordination as a bhikkhu in some orders. Though Sri Lankan orders are often organized along caste lines, men who ordain as monks temporarily pass outside of the conventional caste system, and as such during their time as monks may act (or be treated) in a way that would not be in line with the expected duties and privileges of their caste.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

For those born in Western countries who wish to become Buddhist monks or nuns, it is possible to undertake the lifestyle in their home countries, among other Buddhist monastics in Western countries, or to travel and take up residence in a Buddhist monastery in Asian countries such as Sri Lanka or Thailand. In countries where Buddhism is deeply rooted, it can often be easier to adhere to the lifestyle of a monk or nun, as it requires considerable discipline to successfully live by the non-secular rules and regulations for which Buddhist practices are known. For instance, Theravāda monastics are typically required to abstain from activities such as working, handling money, listening to music and cooking. Such obligations can be especially challenging in non-Buddhist societies.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Some of the more well-known Theravādin monks are [Ajahn Mun](/wiki/Ajahn_Mun "Ajahn Mun"), [Ajahn Chah](/wiki/Ajahn_Chah "Ajahn Chah"), [Ledi Sayadaw](/wiki/Ledi_Sayadaw "Ledi Sayadaw"), [Webu Sayadaw](/wiki/Webu_Sayadaw "Webu Sayadaw"), [Narada Maha Thera](/wiki/Narada_Maha_Thera "Narada Maha Thera"), Ajahn Plien Panyapatipo, [Buddhadasa](/wiki/Buddhadasa "Buddhadasa"), [Mahasi Sayadaw](/wiki/Mahasi_Sayadaw "Mahasi Sayadaw"), [Nyanatiloka Mahathera](/wiki/Nyanatiloka_Mahathera "Nyanatiloka Mahathera"), [Nyanaponika Thera](/wiki/Nyanaponika_Thera "Nyanaponika Thera"), [Preah Maha Ghosananda](/wiki/Preah_Maha_Ghosananda "Preah Maha Ghosananda"), [U Pandita](/wiki/U_Pandita "U Pandita"), [Ajahn Sumedho](/wiki/Ajahn_Sumedho "Ajahn Sumedho"), [Ajahn Khemadhammo](/wiki/Ajahn_Khemadhammo "Ajahn Khemadhammo"), [Bhikkhu Bodhi](/wiki/Bhikkhu_Bodhi "Bhikkhu Bodhi"), [Ajahn Amaro](/wiki/Ajahn_Amaro "Ajahn Amaro"), [Ajahn Sucitto](/wiki/Ajahn_Sucitto "Ajahn Sucitto"), [Ajahn Jayasaro](/wiki/Ajahn_Jayasaro "Ajahn Jayasaro"), [Thanissaro Bhikkhu](/wiki/Thanissaro_Bhikkhu "Thanissaro Bhikkhu"), [Walpola Rahula Thero](/wiki/Walpola_Rahula_Thero "Walpola Rahula Thero"), [Henepola Gunaratana](/wiki/Henepola_Gunaratana "Henepola Gunaratana"), [Bhaddanta Āciṇṇa](/wiki/Bhaddanta_%C4%80ci%E1%B9%87%E1%B9%87a "Bhaddanta Āciṇṇa"), Bhante Yogavacara Rahula, [Luang Pu Sodh Candasaro](/wiki/Luang_Pu_Sodh_Candasaro "Luang Pu Sodh Candasaro"), [K. Sri Dhammananda](/wiki/K._Sri_Dhammananda "K. Sri Dhammananda"), [Sayadaw U Tejaniya](/wiki/Sayadaw_U_Tejaniya "Sayadaw U Tejaniya") and [Bhikkhu Analayo](/wiki/Bhikkhu_Analayo "Bhikkhu Analayo").

### Monastic practices

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Monastic practices")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/EveningPrayers01a.jpg/250px-EveningPrayers01a.jpg)](/wiki/File:EveningPrayers01a.jpg)

A Buddhist Monk chants evening prayers inside a monastery located near the town of [Kantharalak](/wiki/Kantharalak "Kantharalak"), Thailand.

The practices usually vary in different sub-schools and monasteries within Theravāda. But in the most orthodox forest monastery, the monk usually models his practice and lifestyle on that of the Buddha and his first generation of disciples by living close to nature in forest, mountains and caves. Forest monasteries still keep alive the ancient traditions through following the Buddhist monastic code of discipline in all its detail and developing meditation in secluded forests.

In a typical daily routine at the monastery during the 3-month vassa period, the monk will wake up before dawn and will begin the day with group chanting and meditation. At dawn the monks will go out to surrounding villages bare-footed on alms-round and will have the only meal of the day before noon by eating from the bowl by hand. Most of the time is spent on Dhamma study and meditation. Sometimes the abbot or a senior monk will give a Dhamma talk to the visitors. Laity who stay at the monastery will have to abide by the traditional [eight Buddhist precepts](/wiki/Eight_precepts "Eight precepts").

The life of the monk or nun in a community is much more complex than the life of the forest monk. In the Buddhist society of Sri Lanka, most monks spend hours every day in taking care of the needs of lay people such as preaching *bana,*[[216]](#cite_note-228) accepting alms, officiating funerals, teaching *dhamma* to adults and children in addition to providing social services to the community.

After the end of the Vassa period, many of the monks will go out far away from the monastery to find a remote place (usually in the forest) where they can hang their umbrella tents and where it is suitable for the work of self-development. When they go wandering, they walk barefoot, and go wherever they feel inclined. Only those requisites which are necessary will be carried along. These generally consist of the bowl, the three robes, a bathing cloth, an umbrella tent, a mosquito net, a kettle of water, a water filter, razor, sandals, some small candles, and a candle lantern.

The monks do not fix their times for walking and sitting meditation, for as soon as they are free, they just start doing it; nor do they determine for how long they will go on to meditate. Some of them sometimes walk from dusk to dawn whereas at other times they may walk from between two and seven hours. Some may decide to fast for days or stay at dangerous places where ferocious animals live in order to aid their meditation.

Those monks who have been able to achieve a high level of attainment will be able to guide the junior monks and lay Buddhists toward the four degrees of spiritual attainment.

### Bhikkhunis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Bhikkhunis")]

See also: [Women in Buddhism](/wiki/Women_in_Buddhism "Women in Buddhism"), [Ordination of women in Buddhism](/wiki/Ordination_of_women_in_Buddhism "Ordination of women in Buddhism"), and [Criticism of Buddhism § Women in Buddhism](/wiki/Criticism_of_Buddhism#Women_in_Buddhism "Criticism of Buddhism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Dhammananda09.jpg/250px-Dhammananda09.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dhammananda09.jpg)

[Dhammananda Bhikkhuni](/wiki/Dhammananda_Bhikkhuni "Dhammananda Bhikkhuni")

A few years after the arrival of [Mahinda](/wiki/Mahinda_(Buddhist_monk) "Mahinda (Buddhist monk)"), the bhikkhu [Saṅghamittā](/wiki/Sanghamitta "Sanghamitta"), who is also believed to have been the daughter of Ashoka, came to Sri Lanka. She ordained the first nuns in Sri Lanka. In 429, by request of China's emperor, nuns from [Anuradhapura](/wiki/Anuradhapura "Anuradhapura") were sent to China to establish the order there, which subsequently spread across East Asia. The [prātimokṣa](/wiki/Pr%C4%81timok%E1%B9%A3a "Prātimokṣa") of the nun's order in [East Asian Buddhism](/wiki/East_Asian_Buddhism "East Asian Buddhism") is the [Dharmaguptaka](/wiki/Dharmaguptaka "Dharmaguptaka"), which is different from the prātimokṣa of the current Theravāda school; the specific ordination of the early Sangha in Sri Lanka not known, although the Dharmaguptaka sect originated with the Sthāvirīya as well.

The nun's order subsequently died out in Sri Lanka in the 11th century and in Burma in the 13th century. It had already died out around the 10th century in other Theravādin areas. Novice ordination has also disappeared in those countries. Therefore, women who wish to live as renunciates in those countries must do so by taking eight or ten precepts. Neither laywomen nor formally ordained, these women do not receive the recognition, education, financial support or status enjoyed by Buddhist men in their countries. These "precept-holders" live in Myanmar, Cambodia, Laos, Nepal, and Thailand. In particular, the governing council of Burmese Buddhism has ruled that there can be no valid ordination of women in modern times, though some Burmese monks disagree. Japan is a special case as, although it has neither the bhikkhuni nor novice ordinations, the precept-holding nuns who live there do enjoy a higher status and better education than their precept-holder sisters elsewhere, and can even become Zen priests.[[217]](#cite_note-229) In Tibet there is currently no bhikkhuni ordination, but the [Dalai Lama](/wiki/Dalai_Lama "Dalai Lama") has authorized followers of the Tibetan tradition to be ordained as nuns in traditions that have such ordination.

In 1996, 11 selected Sri Lankan women were ordained fully as Theravāda bhikkhunis by a team of Theravāda monks in concert with a team of Korean nuns in India. There is disagreement among Theravāda [vinaya](/wiki/Vinaya "Vinaya") authorities as to whether such ordinations are valid. The [Dambulla](/wiki/Dambulla "Dambulla") chapter of the [Siam Nikaya](/wiki/Siam_Nikaya "Siam Nikaya") in Sri Lanka also carried out a nun's ordination at this time, specifically stating their ordination process was a valid Theravādin process where the other ordination session was not.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalgado2013166–169-230) This chapter has carried out ordination ceremonies for hundreds of nuns since then.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This has been criticized by leading figures in the Siam Nikaya and [Amarapura Nikaya](/wiki/Amarapura_Nikaya "Amarapura Nikaya"), and the governing council of [Buddhism in Myanmar](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Myanmar "Buddhism in Myanmar") has declared that there can be no valid ordination of nuns in modern times, though some Burmese monks disagree with this.[[219]](#cite_note-231)

In 1997 [Dhamma Cetiya Vihara](/w/index.php?title=Dhamma_Cetiya_Vihara&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dhamma Cetiya Vihara (page does not exist)") in Boston was founded by Ven. Gotami of Thailand, then a 10 precept nun; when she received full ordination in 2000, her dwelling became America's first Theravāda Buddhist bhikkhuni vihara.

A 55-year-old Thai Buddhist 8-precept white-robed maechee nun, Varanggana Vanavichayen, became the first woman to receive the going-forth ceremony of a novice (and the gold robe) in Thailand, in 2002.[[220]](#cite_note-232) On 28 February 2003, [Dhammananda Bhikkhuni](/wiki/Dhammananda_Bhikkhuni "Dhammananda Bhikkhuni"), formerly known as Chatsumarn Kabilsingh, became the first Thai woman to receive bhikkhuni ordination as a Theravāda nun.[[221]](#cite_note-thebuddhadharma-233)
Dhammananda Bhikkhuni was ordained in Sri Lanka.[[222]](#cite_note-bhikkhunis-234) The Thai Senate has reviewed and revoked the secular law passed in 1928 banning women's full ordination in Buddhism as unconstitutional for being counter to laws protecting freedom of religion. However, Thailand's two main Theravāda Buddhist orders, the Mahanikaya and Dhammayutika Nikaya, have yet to officially accept fully ordained women into their ranks.

In 2009 in Australia four women received bhikkhuni ordination as Theravāda nuns, the first time such ordination had occurred in Australia.[[223]](#cite_note-235) It was performed in Perth, Australia, on 22 October 2009 at Bodhinyana Monastery. Abbess Vayama together with Venerables Nirodha, Seri, and Hasapanna were ordained as Bhikkhunis by a dual Sangha act of Bhikkhus and Bhikkhunis in full accordance with the Pāli Vinaya.[[224]](#cite_note-236)

In 2010, in the US, four novice nuns were given the full bhikkhuni ordination in the Thai Theravāda tradition, which included the double ordination ceremony. [Henepola Gunaratana](/wiki/Henepola_Gunaratana "Henepola Gunaratana") and other monks and nuns were in attendance. It was the first such ordination ever in the Western hemisphere.[[225]](#cite_note-Boorstein-237)

The first bhikkhuni ordination in Germany, the ordination of German woman Samaneri Dhira, occurred on 21 June 2015 at Anenja Vihara.[[226]](#cite_note-238)

In Indonesia, the first Theravāda ordination of bhikkhunis in Indonesia after more than a thousand years occurred in 2015 at Wisma Kusalayani in Lembang, [Bandung](/wiki/Bandung "Bandung") in [West Java](/wiki/West_Java "West Java").[[227]](#cite_note-bhikkhuni1-239) Those ordained included Vajiradevi Sadhika Bhikkhuni from Indonesia, Medha Bhikkhuni from Sri Lanka, Anula Bhikkhuni from Japan, Santasukha Santamana Bhikkhuni from Vietnam, Sukhi Bhikkhuni and Sumangala Bhikkhuni from Malaysia, and Jenti Bhikkhuni from Australia.[[227]](#cite_note-bhikkhuni1-239)

### Monastic orders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Monastic orders")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Thai_Buddhist_monk_blesses.jpg/250px-Thai_Buddhist_monk_blesses.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thai_Buddhist_monk_blesses.jpg)

Thai monks blessing the [King of Thailand](/wiki/Bhumibol_Adulyadej "Bhumibol Adulyadej") in Wat Nong Wong, [Amphoe Sawankhalok](/wiki/Sawankhalok_District "Sawankhalok District"), [Sukhothai](/wiki/Sukhothai_Province "Sukhothai Province"), Thailand

Theravāda monks typically belong to a particular *[nikaya](/wiki/Nikaya "Nikaya")*, variously referred to as monastic orders or fraternities. These different orders do not typically develop separate doctrines, but may differ in the manner in which they observe monastic rules. These monastic orders represent lineages of ordination, typically tracing their origin to a particular group of monks that established a new ordination tradition within a particular country or geographic area.

In Myanmar, all the monastic orders are called gaing (ဂိုဏ်း) or [gaṇa](/wiki/Gana "Gana") (ဂဏ) instead of nikāya, although some orders include Nikāya (နိကာယ) in addition to Gaing in their names. Currently, it is prohibited by law to establish gaings other than the [nine major gaings](/wiki/Buddhist_sects_in_Myanmar#The_nine_sects_of_Sangha "Buddhist sects in Myanmar") sanctioned in 1980. Other monastic orders and sects have to merge with these nine gaings, while some sects are totally banned.

In Sri Lanka caste plays a major role in the division into nikayas. Some Theravāda Buddhist countries appoint or elect a [sangharaja](/wiki/Sangharaja "Sangharaja"), or Supreme Patriarch of the Sangha, as the highest ranking or seniormost monk in a particular area, or from a particular nikaya. The demise of monarchies has resulted in the suspension of these posts in some countries, but patriarchs have continued to be appointed in Thailand. Cambodia ended the practice of appointing a sangharaja for some time, but the position was later restored, though it lapsed again.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* *[Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh")*:
  + [Mahasthabir Nikaya](/wiki/Mahasthabir_Nikaya "Mahasthabir Nikaya")
  + [Sangharaj Nikaya](/wiki/Sangharaj_Nikaya "Sangharaj Nikaya")
* *[Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar")*:
  + [Thudhamma Gaing](/wiki/Thudhamma_Gaing "Thudhamma Gaing")
  + [Shwegyin Nikāya](/wiki/Shwekyin_Nikaya "Shwekyin Nikaya") Shwegyin Gaing
  + Dhammānudhamma [Mahādvāra Nikāya](/wiki/Mah%C4%81dv%C4%81ra_Nik%C4%81ya "Mahādvāra Nikāya") Gaing
  + Dhammavinayānuloma [Mūladvāra Nikāya](/wiki/M%C5%ABladv%C4%81ra_Nik%C4%81ya "Mūladvāra Nikāya") Gaing
  + Anaukchaung Dvāra Gaing
  + [Veḷuvan Nikāya](/w/index.php?title=Ve%E1%B8%B7uvan_Nik%C4%81ya&action=edit&redlink=1 "Veḷuvan Nikāya (page does not exist)") Gaing
  + Catubhummika Mahāsatipaṭṭhāna [Hngettwin Gaing](/wiki/Hngettwin_Gaing "Hngettwin Gaing")
  + Gaṇavimut Kudo Gaing
  + [Dhammayutti Nikāya](/wiki/Dhammayuttika_Nikaya "Dhammayuttika Nikaya") Mahā Yin Gaing
* *[Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka")*:
  + [Amarapura Nikaya](/wiki/Amarapura_Nikaya "Amarapura Nikaya") has many Sub orders including
    - Dharmarakshitha
    - Kanduboda (or Swejin Nikaya)
    - Tapovana (or Kalyanavamsa)
  + [Ramañña Nikaya](/wiki/Ramanna_Nikaya "Ramanna Nikaya")
    - Delduwa
    - [Sri Kalyani Yogasrama Samstha](/wiki/Sri_Kalyani_Yogasrama_Samstha "Sri Kalyani Yogasrama Samstha") (or 'Galduwa Tradition')
  + [Siam Nikaya](/wiki/Siam_Nikaya "Siam Nikaya")
    - Asgiriya
    - Malwaththa
    - Rohana
    - Waturawila (or Mahavihara Vamshika Shyamopali Vanavasa Nikaya)
* *Thailand and Cambodia*
  + [Dhammayuttika Nikaya](/wiki/Dhammayuttika_Nikaya "Dhammayuttika Nikaya")
  + [Maha Nikaya](/wiki/Maha_Nikaya "Maha Nikaya")

## Impact on modern society

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Theravada&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Impact on modern society")]

Theravada monks meditating and practicing mindfulness over simple things like the meal they are about to have

Theravāda Buddhism's emphasis on mindfulness and meditation practices has influenced modern society by promoting mental well-being and stress reduction techniques leading to the integration of mindfulness in various therapeutic and self-improvement programs. Additionally, its teachings on ethical conduct and compassion continue to inspire individuals and organizations to prioritize moral values and social responsibility in their actions and decision-making.[[228]](#cite_note-240) The emphasis on ethical conduct in Theravada Buddhism encourages individuals to lead virtuous lives. This includes refraining from actions that harm others, such as lying, stealing, and harming living beings. These principles promote personal integrity and honesty in modern society. Buddhism encourages the cultivation of compassion and loving-kindness ([metta](/wiki/Maitr%C4%AB "Maitrī")) towards all sentient beings. In contemporary society, these teachings inspire individuals and organizations to prioritize social responsibility, charitable activities, and humanitarian efforts aimed at alleviating suffering and promoting the welfare of others.[[229]](#cite_note-241) Theravada Buddhism places significant emphasis on self-awareness and self-transformation. Practitioners are encouraged to explore the nature of the self ([anatta](/wiki/Anatt%C4%81 "Anattā")), impermanence ([anicca](/wiki/Anicca "Anicca")), and the nature of suffering ([dukkha](/wiki/Du%E1%B8%A5kha "Duḥkha")). These teachings have inspired individuals in modern society to embark on journeys of self-discovery, self-improvement, and personal growth. The teachings on mindfulness and the impermanence of life have provided individuals with effective tools for coping with stress and life's challenges. In today's fast-paced and often stressful world, these teachings offer valuable strategies for maintaining balance and emotional stability.[[230]](#cite_note-242) This journey of mindfulness and mental health has been strengthened by top institutions encouraging the idea of mindfulness and the effects it has on people. The mindfulness classes/courses/programs provided by many top universities and institutions such as Yale, Stanford, etc. to their students, staff, and general public have benefited them.[[231]](#cite_note-243)

  + [Bangladesh](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Bangladesh "Buddhism in Bangladesh") (by 2% of the population) mainly in [Chittagong Hill Tracts](/wiki/Chittagong_Hill_Tracts "Chittagong Hill Tracts") and Kuwakata, Barishal

  + [Nepal](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Nepal "Buddhism in Nepal")
  + [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Sri_Lanka "Buddhism in Sri Lanka") (by 70% of the population)
* Southeast Asia:
  + [Cambodia](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Cambodia "Buddhism in Cambodia") (by 97% of the population)
  + [Indonesia](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Indonesia "Buddhism in Indonesia")
  + [Laos](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Laos "Buddhism in Laos") (by 66% of the population)
  + [Malaysia](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Malaysia "Buddhism in Malaysia") (in peninsular Malaysia especially north-western parts of Malaysia, primarily by the [Malaysian Siamese](/wiki/Malaysian_Siamese "Malaysian Siamese") and [Malaysian Sinhalese](/wiki/Sri_Lankans_in_Malaysia "Sri Lankans in Malaysia"))
  + [Myanmar](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Myanmar "Buddhism in Myanmar") (by 89% of the population)
  + [Singapore](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Singapore "Buddhism in Singapore")
  + [Thailand](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Thailand "Buddhism in Thailand") (by 90% of the population, 94% of the population that practices religion)
  + [Vietnam](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Vietnam "Buddhism in Vietnam") (by the [Khmer Krom](/wiki/Khmer_Krom "Khmer Krom") in the south and central parts of Vietnam and [Tai Dam](/wiki/Tai_Dam_people "Tai Dam people") in northern Vietnam)
* Theravāda has also recently gained popularity in the [Western world](/wiki/Buddhism_in_the_West "Buddhism in the West").

Today, Theravādins number over 150 million worldwide, and during the past few decades Theravāda Buddhism has begun to [take root in the West](/wiki/Vipassana_movement "Vipassana movement")[[b]](#cite_note-252) and in the [Buddhist revival in India](/wiki/History_of_Buddhism_in_India#Revival_of_Buddhism_in_India "History of Buddhism in India").[[web 20]](#cite_note-253)
